Section 3

Memory & Knowledge Representation

Three distinct stores are required because no single storage technology can serve all access patterns. The decision to separate them is Established practice in data architecture (polyglot persistence). The specific mapping of each store to each memory type is Judgment.

3.1Temporal Event Ledger
→ R1

Why it exists: The system needs an immutable, append-only record of every organizational event in its original, source-attributed form — the "ground truth" from which all other stores are derived. This is the archival layer. It serves as the source of truth for audits, replays, and correction when downstream stores diverge or corrupt.

Enters

Normalized events from the Ingestion layer: structured JSON with source system, actor, timestamp, event type, raw payload, and schema version.

Leaves

Event streams to downstream stores (graph, vector) during initial build and replay. Read by the Audit Log for provenance queries.

Neighbors

Receives from Normalization Pipeline (§4.2). Feeds Semantic Knowledge Graph (§3.2) and Vector Store (§3.3). Read by Audit Log (§6.3).

Rejected Alternative: Using the Semantic Knowledge Graph as the single store of record. Rejected because graphs are mutable by construction — resolving entities and updating relationships means the original signal is lost. An immutable ledger is required for trustworthy audits. Established (event sourcing pattern, Kleppmann 2017).
Failure Mode That Worries Me Most: Silent data loss during webhook ingestion at high volume. If a source system sends an event burst and the connector drops events without detection (no idempotency key checked, no dead-letter queue alerting), the ledger has gaps that are never recoverable. Gap detection must be built into the ingestion layer — not treated as an optional monitoring concern.
3.2Semantic Knowledge Graph Highest Risk
→ R1, R2, R3

Why it exists: The vector store enables semantic similarity lookup but cannot answer relational or temporal questions: "Which engineer last modified the payment module, and were they involved in the incident on Tuesday?" Answering this requires traversal over a typed, attributed graph where entities (people, projects, features, incidents, decisions) and their relationships (authored, depends-on, caused, resolved) are first-class citizens. The knowledge graph is the relational reasoning substrate of the entire system.

This is also the hardest subsystem to build and maintain. Its quality is the primary determinant of system-wide answer quality. It receives the deepest treatment here.

Enters

Enriched, resolved events from the Enrichment Pipeline (§4.3). Entity resolution decisions from the ER Pipeline (§4.2). Human corrections from the Audit Log (§6.3).

Leaves

Subgraphs and entity context to the Reasoning Layer (§5). Relationship-traversal results to the Query Agent. Anomaly signals (disconnected entities, stale nodes) to the Monitor Agent.

Neighbors

Receives from §4.2, §4.3. Serves §5 agents. Read-gated by Access Control (§6.1). Monitored by Observability (§7).

Graph Schema (Core Ontology)

Nodes (entity types): Person — id, name, roles[], team_id, source_system_ids[] Team — id, name, parent_team_id Project — id, name, status, owner_id, created_at Feature — id, title, spec_url, status, priority Decision — id, summary, rationale, alternatives_rejected, made_by, made_at Incident — id, severity, timeline[], resolved_by, root_cause Requirement — id, text, source (customer/internal), linked_features[] Codeunit — id, path, language, last_modified_by, linked_features[] Message — id, channel, author, timestamp, summary, source_type Edges (relationship types, all directed, all timestamped): Person --[authored]--> Codeunit | Decision | Feature | Message Feature --[implements]--> Requirement Feature --[depends_on]--> Feature Decision --[constrains]--> Feature | Project Incident --[affected]--> Feature | Codeunit Person --[member_of]--> Team Message --[references]--> Feature | Decision | Incident | Requirement Feature --[diverges_from]--> Requirement ← R3: misalignment edge

The diverges_from edge is the primary data structure serving R3 (misalignment detection). It is created by the Monitor Agent when it detects that a Feature's implementation scope, as inferred from commits and tickets, materially departs from the linked Requirement or Decision. Judgment — this edge type does not appear in standard knowledge graph schemas.

Construction & Maintenance Strategy

The graph is not manually curated. It is constructed and maintained by four automated processes:

  • Event-driven updates: Every ingested event triggers graph mutations via the Enrichment Pipeline. A new GitHub commit adds or updates an authored edge; a new Jira ticket creates a Feature node; a resolved PagerDuty incident creates an Incident node and potentially an affected edge to the relevant Feature.
  • LLM-assisted edge inference: Periodically, a batch job runs a Message node against an LLM prompt to extract implicit references ("the payment issue from last week" → link to Incident #4412). This is Open Problem territory — precision/recall on implicit references is not yet reliably production-grade.
  • Human correction ingestion: The Audit Log (§6.3) accepts human corrections that propagate to the graph as authoritative edge revisions with provenance.
  • Staleness marking: A scheduled job traverses nodes and marks them stale based on absence of update signals beyond a configurable threshold. Stale nodes are not deleted — they are flagged, and answers drawn from them are uncertainty-qualified.
Rejected Alternative: Using a pure vector store with LLM-based graph reasoning over retrieved text chunks. Rejected because relational questions (traversal, path-finding, aggregation over typed relationships) are not reliably answerable by an LLM reasoning over flat text retrieval. Graph databases (Neo4j, Amazon Neptune, Tigris) provide first-class traversal with typed edge semantics at consistent performance. Established for knowledge graphs; LLM over flat text for relational questions is Open Problem.
Failure Mode That Worries Me Most: Entity resolution errors compounding silently. If "Alice Johnson" in Slack and "ajohnson" in GitHub are never resolved as the same entity, then all authored edges for this person are split across two Person nodes. Queries about "Alice's work" return partial results. The insidious property of this failure is that it looks like correct behavior — you get an answer, but it's incomplete, and you have no signal that it's wrong. This is the single highest-risk failure mode in the entire system. See §4.2 for mitigation.
3.3Vector Retrieval Store
→ R1, R2

Why it exists: Full-text and semantic similarity retrieval over unstructured content — meeting transcripts, Slack threads, PR descriptions, support tickets — cannot be satisfied by graph traversal alone. The vector store handles "find me content semantically related to this query" at sub-second latency across millions of documents. It is the primary retrieval substrate for the Query Agent's RAG pipeline.

Enters

Chunked, embedded documents from the Enrichment Pipeline. Each chunk carries: source_id, entity_refs[], access_control_tags[], timestamp, embedding vector.

Leaves

Top-K semantically similar chunks (with metadata) to the Reasoning Layer. Filtered by access control tags before returning to the requesting agent.

Neighbors

Receives from §4.3. Queried by Query Agent and Synthesis Agent (§5). Access control tags enforced at query time by §6.1.

Rejected Alternative: BM25/Elasticsearch as the sole retrieval layer. Rejected because keyword retrieval misses semantic equivalences critical for cross-tool organizational queries ("checkout bug" vs. "PaymentProcessor NPE"). The design uses hybrid retrieval: dense vector search + sparse BM25, fused with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF). This is Established and consistently outperforms either approach alone.
Failure Mode That Worries Me Most: Embedding model staleness. If the organization adopts new domain-specific terminology (a new product name, a new technical acronym, a strategic initiative) and the embedding model was trained before that vocabulary existed, semantic search will fail to retrieve relevant documents for queries using the new terms. This is silent — no error, just irrelevant results. Mitigation: custom fine-tuning on org-specific vocabulary, or retrieval augmented with BM25 as fallback for exact-match terms.
Section 4

Ingestion & Processing

4.1Connector Fleet
→ R1

Why it exists: Each source system has a different API contract, rate limit, authentication model, and event schema. The Connector Fleet isolates this heterogeneity, presenting a uniform event stream to downstream components. Without isolation here, every downstream pipeline stage would need source-specific logic — creating n×m complexity.

Enters

Raw API responses, webhook payloads, or CDC event streams from source systems. Authenticated per-source via OAuth 2.0, API key, or service account credentials.

Leaves

Raw events written to the message broker (Kafka/Kinesis) with envelope: {source, event_type, raw_payload, received_at, idempotency_key}.

Neighbors

Pulls from source systems. Pushes to message broker. Managed by Connector Health dashboard in §7.

Key design decisions: Idempotency keys on every event prevent duplicate processing during retries. A dead-letter queue (DLQ) captures events that fail normalization for manual inspection. Connectors are isolated processes — a failing Salesforce connector does not affect GitHub ingestion. Rate-limit budgets are managed per-connector with exponential backoff.

Rejected Alternative: Using a commercial iPaaS (Zapier, Workato, Boomi) for connectivity. Rejected because iPaaS tools are optimized for action automation, not high-volume event streaming with observable data quality. They also introduce a vendor-controlled data path that creates compliance complexity. Airbyte (open source) is the preferred foundation for batch connectors; custom lightweight webhook receivers for real-time event connectors.
Failure Mode: Source system API schema changes silently break connectors, and the DLQ fills without alerting. Mitigation: schema validation on every event at connector egress with immediate alerting on validation failure rate exceeding threshold.
4.2Normalization & Entity Resolution Pipeline High Risk
→ R1

Why it exists: Raw events from different systems use incompatible schemas and identity namespaces. A "user" in Salesforce and a "user" in GitHub may be the same person — this must be resolved before knowledge is stored, or the graph fragments into disconnected islands that make cross-tool reasoning impossible. This pipeline is the semantic foundation of the entire system.

Enters

Raw events from the Connector Fleet via message broker. Each event carries source-system identity fields (email, username, user ID, display name).

Leaves

Normalized events with resolved canonical entity IDs replacing all source-local identifiers. Written to the Temporal Event Ledger and forwarded to the Enrichment Pipeline.

Neighbors

Receives from §4.1. Writes to §3.1 (Ledger). Maintains the Entity Resolution Index — a separate mapping store of {source_system_id → canonical_id}.

Entity Resolution Strategy

Entity resolution (ER) is solved in two stages: deterministic matching first, probabilistic matching second.

  • Deterministic: Match on email address (normalized to lowercase) across systems. If Slack user email matches GitHub verified email, they are resolved with 100% confidence. This covers the majority of person-entity resolution in most orgs.
  • Probabilistic: Where email is absent or inconsistent, match on display name similarity (normalized Levenshtein distance), username pattern matching, and temporal co-occurrence of activity. Matches below a confidence threshold (tunable, default 0.85) are flagged as candidate merges and routed to a human review queue rather than auto-resolved.
  • Human review queue: A lightweight UI surfaces candidate merges for a designated data steward to confirm or reject. Confirmed merges write to the Entity Resolution Index and trigger a graph update. This is critical — ER errors are the most damaging class of data quality failure in the system.
Rejected Alternative: Fully automated LLM-based entity resolution for all cases. Rejected because LLM-based ER has non-deterministic behavior and can confidently merge distinct entities (two people named "Alex Chen" in different teams). For a system where downstream answers depend on entity identity, ER errors must be bounded and correctable. The hybrid deterministic + human-in-the-loop approach is Judgment — fully automated ER with human audit is an alternative for higher-volume organizations.
Failure Mode That Worries Me Most: Two real individuals who share a display name and similar email patterns are merged into one canonical entity. All queries about either person now return a confused mix of their activities. This is not just a data quality error — it is a privacy violation if one person's work becomes visible to queries targeting the other. Mitigation: the confidence threshold for auto-merge must be conservative; merge operations must be reversible and logged.
4.3Enrichment & Classification Pipeline
→ R1, R3

Why it exists: Normalized events carry structural identity but lack semantic classification. A Jira ticket status change does not carry a label saying "this is evidence of feature X drifting from requirement Y." The enrichment pipeline adds semantic meaning: topic classification, sentiment signals, cross-entity reference extraction, and misalignment signal scoring. This is what transforms raw organizational events into intelligence-bearing artifacts.

Enters

Normalized, entity-resolved events from §4.2.

Leaves

Enriched events to the Knowledge Graph (new/updated nodes and edges) and chunked, embedded documents to the Vector Store.

Neighbors

Receives from §4.2. Writes to §3.2 and §3.3. Classification models monitored by §7.

Key enrichments: Entity cross-reference extraction (LLM-assisted, identifies mentions of other system entities in free text); topic classification (lightweight classifier mapping content to org-defined topic taxonomy); misalignment signal scoring (heuristic rules + LLM evaluation detecting scope drift, delayed delivery signals, unresolved blocking dependencies); and PII scrubbing before storage (regex + NER-based redaction of personal data not required for organizational intelligence purposes). Judgment — the specific enrichments are configurable; this list represents a recommended baseline.

Failure Mode: LLM-based cross-reference extraction introduces spurious edges into the Knowledge Graph. A message saying "we need to fix this like the payment issue from Q2" creates a false reference edge to Incident #4412 when the writer meant a different incident. False positive edges cause misleading answers to relational queries. Mitigation: reference extraction confidence threshold; cross-reference edges marked with confidence score and displayed with caveats in answers.
Section 5 — Deep Treatment (Highest Risk Subsystem)

Reasoning & Agents

This is the highest-risk subsystem in the entire design. It is where answers are generated, where hallucination can occur, where agent errors can compound, and where user trust is earned or permanently lost. It receives disproportionate depth here.

The question "how many distinct agent classes does this system truly need?" is asked before naming any agents. The answer is derived from the system's responsibilities, not from a desire to demonstrate agentic complexity.

5.1 Four Agent Classes — Derived from Responsibilities

Each agent class is justified by a distinct cognitive task that cannot be collapsed into another without creating an agent with conflicting objectives. Judgment — a monolithic "reasoning service" is the alternative (simpler, less robust); the four-agent split is justified by the quality attribute of verifiability.

Agent ClassResponsibilityInputsOutputsCannot Be Merged With
QueryAgent R2 — Answer questions accurately on demand User query + session identity. Retrieves context from Vector Store and Knowledge Graph via hybrid retrieval. Draft answer + retrieved source list + confidence estimate. Passed to VerificationAgent before delivery. MonitorAgent — mixing reactive answering with proactive monitoring creates objective conflicts and makes testing harder.
MonitorAgent R3 — Detect misalignment proactively Scheduled triggers + change events from the Knowledge Graph. Compares Feature nodes against linked Requirement/Decision nodes. Misalignment alerts (typed: scope drift, delivery delay, contradiction detected, orphaned requirement). Written to an alert queue and delivered to the Interface Layer. QueryAgent — proactive monitoring requires persistent state and scheduled execution; mixing with on-demand query serving creates resource contention and unclear restart semantics.
SynthesisAgent R2, R3 — Produce complex multi-source summaries and structured drafts Orchestrated by QueryAgent or MonitorAgent when a request exceeds single-retrieval complexity. Receives a synthesis task specification including sources, output format, and scope constraints. Structured artifacts: decision summaries, spec drafts, incident timelines, cross-team briefings. Always includes full source attribution. VerificationAgent — separating synthesis from verification ensures the verifier has no stake in the answer it is checking.
VerificationAgent R2, R5 — Check every answer before delivery Draft answer or synthesis artifact from QueryAgent/SynthesisAgent + the exact retrieved sources used. Verification verdict: {PASS, UNCERTAIN, FAIL} with specific annotation. PASS → deliver to user. UNCERTAIN → deliver with explicit uncertainty caveat. FAIL → block delivery and log for investigation. Any other agent — the VerificationAgent's independence is its entire value. It must have no shared state with the agent whose output it is checking.
On Not Having More Agents "CoordinatorAgent," "PlannerAgent," "MemoryAgent," and "RouterAgent" were all considered and rejected. Each responsibility they would hold is either already handled by the four agents above or belongs in the infrastructure layer (routing is a message broker concern, not an agent concern). Adding agents increases coordination overhead, makes debugging harder, and creates more failure surfaces. The design bias is: fewer agents, each with a precisely bounded responsibility.

5.2 Inter-Agent Communication Protocol

Agents do not call each other directly. All inter-agent communication passes through a shared task queue (backed by the same message broker used in ingestion). This is Established — direct coupling between agents creates circular dependency risks and makes independent scaling impossible.

Task Queue Protocol: { "task_id": "uuid", "task_type": "QUERY | SYNTHESIS | VERIFY | MONITOR_CHECK", "originator": "agent_class | user_id", "priority": 1-5, "payload": { ... task-specific ... }, "context": { "user_identity": "canonical_user_id", "access_scopes": ["team:engineering", "project:auth-service"], "session_id": "uuid", "parent_task_id": "uuid | null" ← enables task chain tracing }, "deadline_ms": 5000, "audit_required": true } Routing: User query received → Task{QUERY} → QueryAgent → If multi-source → Task{SYNTHESIS} → SynthesisAgent → Answer draft → Task{VERIFY} → VerificationAgent → PASS → Interface Layer → User → UNCERTAIN → Interface Layer → User (with caveat) → FAIL → Dead-letter + Alert → Human Reviewer
arguments.title: AI OS for Companies — Principal Reference Architecture

data.content: Created artifact "AI OS for Companies — Principal Reference Architecture" (id: ai_os_reference_architecture).

arguments.description: Principal reference architecture for the AI OS for Companies. Responsibility-first design tracing 11 subsystems to 5 core obligations, with deep treatment of Reasoning/Agents and Security/Governance, honest weakness analysis, and full assumption log.
arguments.html:
Principal Reference Architecture  ·  Enterprise AI Systems

AI Operating System for Companies

A responsibility-first reference architecture. Every subsystem derives from a stated fundamental responsibility and may be cut if it cannot. Design decisions are explained, alternatives rejected are named, failure modes are stated plainly, and weaknesses are not glossed over.

Building on: Research report (Parts 1–2) — problem space, five structural barriers, AI foundations. Not re-derived here. Target scope: Medium-to-large org, 500–10,000 employees, 10–60 integrated source systems. Legend: Established = well-proven pattern   Judgment = author's design call   High Risk = warrants extra scrutiny   Open Problem = no settled solution
Contents
  1. The Five Fundamental Responsibilities
  2. System Overview & Traceability Map
  3. Memory & Knowledge Representation
    • 3.1 Temporal Event Ledger
    • 3.2 Semantic Knowledge Graph
    • 3.3 Vector Retrieval Store
  4. Ingestion & Processing
    • 4.1 Connector Fleet
    • 4.2 Normalization & Entity Resolution Pipeline
    • 4.3 Enrichment & Classification Pipeline
  5. Reasoning & Agents — Deep Treatment
    • 5.1 Four Agent Classes
    • 5.2 Inter-Agent Communication Protocol
    • 5.3 Human Supervision Architecture
    • 5.4 Faithfulness Verification Subsystem
  6. Security & Governance — Deep Treatment
    • 6.1 Identity-Bound Access Control
    • 6.2 Conflict & Contradiction Detection
    • 6.3 Audit & Correction Log
  7. Observability & Evaluation
  8. Infrastructure & Deployment
  9. Honest Weakness Analysis
  10. Explicit Assumption Log
Section 1

The Five Fundamental Responsibilities

These are not features. They are irreducible obligations. Every subsystem in this document traces to at least one. Components that do not trace here are cut.

R1

Capture & Preserve Organizational Memory

Continuously ingest, normalize, and durably store the explicit signals an organization produces — decisions, conversations, work artifacts, operational events — in a form retrievable and reasoned over indefinitely.

R2

Answer Organizational Questions Accurately

Respond to natural-language queries about organizational state — past, present, inferred — with answers that are accurate, cited, and calibrated for uncertainty. The system must know and communicate what it does not know.

R3

Detect & Surface Misalignment Proactively

Continuously monitor the relationship between stated intent (OKRs, specs, decisions) and observed execution (commits, deployments, customer signals) and alert when material divergence is detected — without being asked.

R4

Enforce Information Access Rights

Guarantee that no actor — human or agent — accesses information, generates answers, or triggers actions beyond explicitly granted authority. The system must be at least as safe to query as the underlying source systems.

R5

Be Continuously Self-Auditable

Provide sufficient observability that any stakeholder can determine: what the system knows, how it reached any conclusion, whether knowledge is stale, and whether any component is degrading silently.

Responsibility Deliberately Excluded "Execute autonomous actions on behalf of users" was considered and excluded. Autonomous action requires independent safety architecture and organizational trust maturity that a first-generation deployment cannot assume. The reasoning layer (§5) supports supervised action preparation — generating draft specs, flagging drift — but stops at autonomous execution. This is a Judgment call; teams with higher trust maturity may revisit it as a Phase 2 scope extension.
Section 2

System Overview & Traceability Map

╔═══════════════════════════════════════════╗ ║ SOURCE SYSTEMS (not owned) ║ ║ Slack · GitHub · Linear · Notion · Gong ║ ║ Salesforce · Zoom · Datadog · Calendar ║ ╚══════════════╤════════════════════════════╝ │ webhooks · CDC · REST polls ▼ ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ R1 │ INGESTION & PROCESSING (§4) │ │ Connector Fleet → Normalization/Entity Resolution │ │ → Enrichment & Classification │ └──────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────┘ │ normalized, attributed events ▼ ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ R1, R4 │ MEMORY & KNOWLEDGE REPRESENTATION (§3) │ │ ┌─────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌────────────┐ │ │ │ Temporal │ │ Semantic │ │ Vector │ │ │ │ Event │ │ Knowledge │ │ Retrieval │ │ │ │ Ledger │ │ Graph │ │ Store │ │ │ └─────────────┘ └──────────────┘ └────────────┘ │ │ ◄── Access Control Enforced at Read ──► │ └──────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────┘ │ authorized, attributed context ▼ ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ R2, R3 │ REASONING & AGENTS (§5) │ │ │ │ QueryAgent MonitorAgent SynthesisAgent │ │ ↕ │ │ VerificationAgent (checks all three) │ │ │ │ ════ human supervision boundary ════ │ └──────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────┘ │ responses · alerts · drafts ▼ ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ INTERFACE LAYER │ │ Chat API · Alert Webhooks · Draft Artifacts · SDK│ └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ╔════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗ R4 ║ SECURITY & GOVERNANCE (§6) — cross-cutting ║ ║ Identity Binding · Conflict Resolution · ║ ║ Audit & Correction Log ║ ╚════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝ ╔════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗ R5 ║ OBSERVABILITY & EVALUATION (§7) — cross-cutting ║ ║ Pipeline Health · Retrieval Quality · ║ ║ Faithfulness Telemetry · Knowledge Freshness ║ ╚════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
SubsystemResponsibility(ies)SectionRisk Level
Temporal Event LedgerR1§3.1Medium
Semantic Knowledge GraphR1, R2, R3§3.2High
Vector Retrieval StoreR1, R2§3.3Medium
Connector FleetR1§4.1Medium
Normalization & Entity ResolutionR1§4.2High
Enrichment & ClassificationR1, R3§4.3Medium
Reasoning & Agents (4 classes)R2, R3§5Highest
Identity-Bound Access ControlR4§6.1High
Conflict & Contradiction DetectionR2, R5§6.2Medium
Audit & Correction LogR4, R5§6.3Medium
Observability & EvaluationR5§7Medium
Section 3

Memory & Knowledge Representation

Three distinct stores are required because no single storage technology can serve all access patterns. The decision to separate them is Established practice in data architecture (polyglot persistence). The specific mapping of each store to each memory type is Judgment.

3.1Temporal Event Ledger
→ R1

Why it exists: The system needs an immutable, append-only record of every organizational event in its original, source-attributed form — the "ground truth" from which all other stores are derived. This is the archival layer. It serves as the source of truth for audits, replays, and correction when downstream stores diverge or corrupt.

Enters

Normalized events from the Ingestion layer: structured JSON with source system, actor, timestamp, event type, raw payload, and schema version.

Leaves

Event streams to downstream stores (graph, vector) during initial build and replay. Read by the Audit Log for provenance queries.

Neighbors

Receives from Normalization Pipeline (§4.2). Feeds Semantic Knowledge Graph (§3.2) and Vector Store (§3.3). Read by Audit Log (§6.3).

Rejected Alternative: Using the Semantic Knowledge Graph as the single store of record. Rejected because graphs are mutable by construction — resolving entities and updating relationships means the original signal is lost. An immutable ledger is required for trustworthy audits. Established (event sourcing pattern, Kleppmann 2017).
Failure Mode That Worries Me Most: Silent data loss during webhook ingestion at high volume. If a source system sends an event burst and the connector drops events without detection (no idempotency key checked, no dead-letter queue alerting), the ledger has gaps that are never recoverable. Gap detection must be built into the ingestion layer — not treated as an optional monitoring concern.
3.2Semantic Knowledge Graph Highest Risk
→ R1, R2, R3

Why it exists: The vector store enables semantic similarity lookup but cannot answer relational or temporal questions: "Which engineer last modified the payment module, and were they involved in the incident on Tuesday?" Answering this requires traversal over a typed, attributed graph where entities (people, projects, features, incidents, decisions) and their relationships (authored, depends-on, caused, resolved) are first-class citizens. The knowledge graph is the relational reasoning substrate of the entire system.

This is also the hardest subsystem to build and maintain. Its quality is the primary determinant of system-wide answer quality. It receives the deepest treatment here.

Enters

Enriched, resolved events from the Enrichment Pipeline (§4.3). Entity resolution decisions from the ER Pipeline (§4.2). Human corrections from the Audit Log (§6.3).

Leaves

Subgraphs and entity context to the Reasoning Layer (§5). Relationship-traversal results to the Query Agent. Anomaly signals (disconnected entities, stale nodes) to the Monitor Agent.

Neighbors

Receives from §4.2, §4.3. Serves §5 agents. Read-gated by Access Control (§6.1). Monitored by Observability (§7).

Graph Schema (Core Ontology)

Nodes (entity types): Person — id, name, roles[], team_id, source_system_ids[] Team — id, name, parent_team_id Project — id, name, status, owner_id, created_at Feature — id, title, spec_url, status, priority Decision — id, summary, rationale, alternatives_rejected, made_by, made_at Incident — id, severity, timeline[], resolved_by, root_cause Requirement — id, text, source (customer/internal), linked_features[] Codeunit — id, path, language, last_modified_by, linked_features[] Message — id, channel, author, timestamp, summary, source_type Edges (relationship types, all directed, all timestamped): Person --[authored]--> Codeunit | Decision | Feature | Message Feature --[implements]--> Requirement Feature --[depends_on]--> Feature Decision --[constrains]--> Feature | Project Incident --[affected]--> Feature | Codeunit Person --[member_of]--> Team Message --[references]--> Feature | Decision | Incident | Requirement Feature --[diverges_from]--> Requirement ← R3: misalignment edge

The diverges_from edge is the primary data structure serving R3 (misalignment detection). It is created by the Monitor Agent when it detects that a Feature's implementation scope, as inferred from commits and tickets, materially departs from the linked Requirement or Decision. Judgment — this edge type does not appear in standard knowledge graph schemas.

Construction & Maintenance Strategy

The graph is not manually curated. It is constructed and maintained by four automated processes:

  • Event-driven updates: Every ingested event triggers graph mutations via the Enrichment Pipeline. A new GitHub commit adds or updates an authored edge; a new Jira ticket creates a Feature node; a resolved PagerDuty incident creates an Incident node and potentially an affected edge to the relevant Feature.
  • LLM-assisted edge inference: Periodically, a batch job runs a Message node against an LLM prompt to extract implicit references ("the payment issue from last week" → link to Incident #4412). This is Open Problem territory — precision/recall on implicit references is not yet reliably production-grade.
  • Human correction ingestion: The Audit Log (§6.3) accepts human corrections that propagate to the graph as authoritative edge revisions with provenance.
  • Staleness marking: A scheduled job traverses nodes and marks them stale based on absence of update signals beyond a configurable threshold. Stale nodes are not deleted — they are flagged, and answers drawn from them are uncertainty-qualified.
Rejected Alternative: Using a pure vector store with LLM-based graph reasoning over retrieved text chunks. Rejected because relational questions (traversal, path-finding, aggregation over typed relationships) are not reliably answerable by an LLM reasoning over flat text retrieval. Graph databases (Neo4j, Amazon Neptune, Tigris) provide first-class traversal with typed edge semantics at consistent performance. Established for knowledge graphs; LLM over flat text for relational questions is Open Problem.
Failure Mode That Worries Me Most: Entity resolution errors compounding silently. If "Alice Johnson" in Slack and "ajohnson" in GitHub are never resolved as the same entity, then all authored edges for this person are split across two Person nodes. Queries about "Alice's work" return partial results. The insidious property of this failure is that it looks like correct behavior — you get an answer, but it's incomplete, and you have no signal that it's wrong. This is the single highest-risk failure mode in the entire system. See §4.2 for mitigation.
3.3Vector Retrieval Store
→ R1, R2

Why it exists: Full-text and semantic similarity retrieval over unstructured content — meeting transcripts, Slack threads, PR descriptions, support tickets — cannot be satisfied by graph traversal alone. The vector store handles "find me content semantically related to this query" at sub-second latency across millions of documents. It is the primary retrieval substrate for the Query Agent's RAG pipeline.

Enters

Chunked, embedded documents from the Enrichment Pipeline. Each chunk carries: source_id, entity_refs[], access_control_tags[], timestamp, embedding vector.

Leaves

Top-K semantically similar chunks (with metadata) to the Reasoning Layer. Filtered by access control tags before returning to the requesting agent.

Neighbors

Receives from §4.3. Queried by Query Agent and Synthesis Agent (§5). Access control tags enforced at query time by §6.1.

Rejected Alternative: BM25/Elasticsearch as the sole retrieval layer. Rejected because keyword retrieval misses semantic equivalences critical for cross-tool organizational queries ("checkout bug" vs. "PaymentProcessor NPE"). The design uses hybrid retrieval: dense vector search + sparse BM25, fused with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF). This is Established and consistently outperforms either approach alone.
Failure Mode That Worries Me Most: Embedding model staleness. If the organization adopts new domain-specific terminology (a new product name, a new technical acronym, a strategic initiative) and the embedding model was trained before that vocabulary existed, semantic search will fail to retrieve relevant documents for queries using the new terms. This is silent — no error, just irrelevant results. Mitigation: custom fine-tuning on org-specific vocabulary, or retrieval augmented with BM25 as fallback for exact-match terms.

4.1Connector Fleet
→ R1

Reasoning & Agents
