Section 3

Memory & Knowledge Representation

Three distinct stores are required because no single storage technology can optimally serve all access patterns simultaneously. This separation is Established polyglot persistence. The specific assignment of organizational memory types to storage technologies is Judgment.

3.1Temporal Event Ledger
→ R1

Why it exists: The system needs an immutable, append-only record of every organizational event in its original, source-attributed form. This is the archival ground truth from which all other stores are derived and to which they can be reconciled when errors occur. Without this, there is no recovery path when the Knowledge Graph or Vector Store are corrupted or when entity resolution decisions need to be revised retroactively.

Enters

Normalized events from the Ingestion layer. Each event carries: source_system, event_type, actor_id (pre-resolution), timestamp, raw_payload, schema_version, idempotency_key.

Leaves

Event replay streams to Knowledge Graph and Vector Store during initial build, schema migration, or error recovery. Provenance records to Audit Log (§6.3).

Neighbors

Receives from Normalization Pipeline (§4.2). Feeds Knowledge Graph (§3.2), Vector Store (§3.3) via replay consumer. Read by Audit Log (§6.3). Monitored by §7.

Technology: Append-only object storage (S3-compatible) with Parquet partitioned by source and date for analytical replay, plus a streaming log (Kafka with long retention) for real-time downstream consumption. Established — this is the Lambda Architecture's batch layer applied to organizational events.

Rejected Alternative: Using the Knowledge Graph as the store of record. Rejected because graphs are mutable by construction — entity resolution merges and relationship updates rewrite history. An immutable ledger is required for audits and for rebuilding downstream stores from scratch when errors accumulate. The ledger-as-source-of-truth pattern is Established (event sourcing, Kleppmann 2017).
Failure Mode That Worries Me Most: Silent event loss during webhook ingestion at high volume. If a source system sends an event burst and the connector drops events without detection — no idempotency key check, no dead-letter queue alert — the ledger has permanent gaps that downstream stores will silently reflect. Gap detection (event sequence monitoring per source) must be built into the ingestion layer as a first-class concern, not deferred to monitoring.
3.2Semantic Knowledge Graph High Risk
→ R1, R2, R3

Why it exists: The vector store enables semantic similarity lookup but cannot answer relational or structural questions: "Which engineers last modified the payment module, and were any of them involved in Tuesday's incident?" Answering this requires traversal over a typed, attributed graph where entities and their relationships are first-class citizens. The Knowledge Graph is the relational reasoning substrate — and the primary determinant of system-wide answer quality. Its correctness is the single most important data quality concern in the entire architecture.

Enters

Entity and relationship mutations from the Enrichment Pipeline (§4.3). Entity resolution decisions from §4.2. Human corrections from Audit Log (§6.3).

Leaves

Subgraphs and traversal results to Reasoning agents (§5). Misalignment signals (diverges_from edges) to MonitorAgent. Entity context to Vector Store for metadata filtering.

Neighbors

Receives from §4.2, §4.3. Serves all §5 agents. All reads gated by Access Control (§6.1). Staleness signals published to Observability (§7).

Core Ontology — Node & Edge Types

NODE TYPES (with key attributes): Person {id, canonical_email, display_names[], roles[], team_ids[], source_ids{}} Team {id, name, parent_team_id, area} Project {id, name, status, owner_id, timeline{start,end}} Feature {id, title, status, priority, spec_url, linked_requirements[]} Requirement {id, text, source_type, customer_segment, priority} Decision {id, summary, rationale, alternatives_rejected[], made_by[], made_at, superseded_by} Incident {id, severity, status, timeline[], root_cause, affected_features[]} Codeunit {id, path, language, last_modified_by, linked_features[]} Message {id, channel_type, author_id, timestamp, summary, source_system} EDGE TYPES (directed, typed, timestamped, confidence-scored): Person --[authored]--> Codeunit | Decision | Feature | Message | Requirement Feature --[implements]--> Requirement Feature --[depends_on]--> Feature Decision --[constrains]--> Feature | Project Incident --[affected]--> Feature | Codeunit Message --[references]--> Feature | Decision | Incident | Requirement | Person Person --[member_of]--> Team (with valid_from, valid_until for org change tracking) Feature --[diverges_from]--> Requirement | Decision ← PRIMARY R3 signal edge KEY DESIGN DECISIONS: All edges carry: {created_at, source_event_id, confidence: 0.0–1.0, created_by: pipeline|human} Edges with confidence < 0.7 are stored but excluded from answers unless explicitly requested. The diverges_from edge is created by MonitorAgent, not by the pipeline — it is an inference, not a direct event signal. This distinction is surfaced in all answers citing it.

Graph Construction & Maintenance

The graph is not manually curated. It is maintained by four automated processes running at different cadences:

  • Real-time event-driven updates: Every ingested event triggers graph mutations via the Enrichment Pipeline. A GitHub commit → authored edge; a new Jira ticket → Feature node; a PagerDuty resolution → Incident node closure + affected edges. Latency target: <60 seconds from source event to graph update.
  • Scheduled LLM-assisted edge inference: A batch job (hourly) runs Message nodes through an LLM extraction prompt to surface implicit cross-references ("the auth issue last sprint" → link to Feature #812). Results stored as low-confidence edges pending confirmation. Open Problem — precision/recall on implicit organizational references is not yet reliably production-grade at scale.
  • Human correction ingestion: The Audit Log (§6.3) surfaces a correction UI for erroneous nodes or edges. Human corrections propagate to the graph as authoritative overrides with provenance and are reflected in the Temporal Event Ledger as correction events.
  • Scheduled staleness marking: A daily job marks nodes stale when their last-updated timestamp exceeds a configurable threshold (default: 30 days for Decisions, 7 days for Feature status). Stale nodes are not deleted — they are flagged, and any answer drawing on them is uncertainty-qualified automatically.
Rejected Alternative A: Using a pure vector store with LLM-based relational reasoning over retrieved text chunks. Rejected because relational questions (graph traversal, typed path-finding, aggregation over relationship types) are not reliably answerable by LLM reasoning over flat retrieval. The error rate on multi-hop relational questions without a graph structure is unacceptably high for an enterprise trust model. Established: knowledge graphs for relational reasoning are proven at Google (KG), LinkedIn (Economic Graph), Microsoft (MSR KG).
Rejected Alternative B: A single mega-graph storing all organizational data in a commercial knowledge graph platform (e.g., Stardog, Cambridge Semantics). Rejected because ontology lock-in to a proprietary schema makes future schema evolution costly. Preferred technology: Neo4j (open-source core) or Amazon Neptune with a custom ontology layer. Judgment.
Failure Mode That Worries Me Most: Entity resolution errors compounding silently. If "Alice Johnson" in Slack and "ajohnson" in GitHub are never resolved to the same Person node, all authored edges for this individual are split. Queries about "Alice's work" return partial results that look complete. The critical property: this failure produces no error signal — the system returns an answer with apparent confidence. This is the highest-risk silent failure mode in the entire system. Mitigation strategy detailed in §4.2.
3.3Vector Retrieval Store
→ R1, R2

Why it exists: Full-text and semantic similarity retrieval over unstructured organizational content — meeting transcripts, Slack threads, PR descriptions, support tickets, design docs — cannot be satisfied by graph traversal. The Vector Store handles "find me content semantically related to this concept" at sub-second latency across millions of documents. It is the primary retrieval substrate for the QueryAgent's RAG pipeline.

Enters

Chunked, embedded documents from the Enrichment Pipeline. Each chunk carries: source_id, entity_refs[], access_control_tags[], timestamp, embedding_vector, chunk_type, staleness_flag.

Leaves

Top-K semantically similar chunks with full metadata to the Reasoning Layer. Access control tags enforced before returning results to the requesting agent's identity context.

Neighbors

Receives from §4.3. Queried by QueryAgent and SynthesisAgent (§5). Access control enforced by §6.1 at query time. Monitored by §7 for retrieval quality degradation.

Retrieval strategy: Hybrid — dense vector search (ANN over embeddings) + sparse BM25/keyword search, fused via Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF). Established — hybrid retrieval consistently outperforms either approach alone across organizational content benchmarks. Technology: Weaviate or Qdrant for dense; Elasticsearch for sparse; RRF fusion in the QueryAgent retrieval planner.

Rejected Alternative: A single dense-only vector database. Rejected because BM25 outperforms dense search for exact-match queries on organizational identifiers: ticket numbers, feature names, commit hashes, employee names. Dense-only systems fail on queries like "show me everything about PROJ-4412." Hybrid is slightly more complex but the retrieval quality improvement is not marginal — it is substantial for organizational content. Established.
Failure Mode That Worries Me Most: Embedding model vocabulary staleness. When the organization adopts new terminology — a new product name, strategic initiative, or technical concept — that the embedding model was not trained on, semantic search silently fails to retrieve relevant documents. No error is raised; results are just irrelevant. Mitigation: hybrid retrieval with BM25 as fallback for terms absent from embedding vocabulary; organization-specific vocabulary fine-tuning for embedding models on a quarterly cadence. This is Open Problem for continuous vocabulary evolution.
Section 4

Ingestion & Processing

4.1Connector Fleet
→ R1

Why it exists: Each source system has a different API contract, rate limit, authentication model, event schema, and retry behavior. The Connector Fleet isolates this heterogeneity behind a uniform event stream interface. Without this isolation, every downstream processing stage would embed source-specific logic, creating n×m coupling complexity that makes adding new integrations prohibitively expensive.

Enters

Raw API responses, webhook payloads, or CDC event streams. Authenticated per-source via OAuth 2.0, API key, or service account credentials stored in a secrets manager (not in the connector config).

Leaves

Raw events written to the message broker (Kafka topics, one per source-system type) with envelope: {source, event_type, raw_payload, received_at, idempotency_key, schema_version}.

Neighbors

Pulls/receives from source systems. Pushes to message broker. Connector health metrics published to Observability (§7). Credential rotation managed externally via secrets manager.

Key implementation decisions: Connectors are isolated processes — a failing Salesforce connector does not affect GitHub ingestion. Established: process isolation for connector fleets is standard in data platform engineering. Idempotency keys prevent duplicate processing on retries. A dead-letter queue (DLQ) captures events failing schema validation. Rate-limit budgets are managed per-connector with token-bucket throttling and exponential backoff. All secrets are injected at runtime, never stored in connector configuration files.

Rejected Alternative: Commercial iPaaS (Zapier, Workato, Boomi). Rejected because iPaaS tools are optimized for action orchestration, not high-volume observable event streaming. They also introduce a vendor-controlled data path that creates compliance complexity (data leaves the org's control boundary to route through a third party). Airbyte (open-source) is preferred for batch connectors; lightweight custom webhook receivers for real-time connectors. Judgment — iPaaS may be appropriate for very early-stage deployments where speed of connector coverage outweighs data governance concerns.
Failure Mode: Source system API schema changes silently break connectors, causing events to fail validation and accumulate in the DLQ without detection. Mitigation: schema validation on every event at connector egress with immediate alerting when validation failure rate exceeds 1% over any 15-minute window.
4.2Normalization & Entity Resolution Pipeline High Risk
→ R1

Why it exists: Raw events from different systems use incompatible schemas and identity namespaces. "user_id: sf_0048X" in Salesforce and "login: ajohnson" in GitHub may be the same person — this must be resolved before knowledge is stored, or the Knowledge Graph fragments into disconnected islands making cross-tool reasoning impossible. This pipeline is the semantic foundation of the entire system. Its errors are the most damaging and the hardest to detect.

Enters

Raw events from the Connector Fleet via message broker. Each carries source-system identity fields: email addresses, usernames, user IDs, display names, project identifiers.

Leaves

Normalized events with all source-local identifiers replaced by canonical entity IDs. Written to the Temporal Event Ledger (§3.1) and forwarded downstream. Also writes to the Entity Resolution Index (a separate mapping store).

Neighbors

Receives from §4.1. Writes to §3.1 (Ledger). Maintains the Entity Resolution Index. Sends candidate merges to the Human Review Queue. Monitored by §7 for resolution confidence distribution.

Entity Resolution Strategy

ER is solved in two stages, applied in sequence. The goal is to maximize auto-resolution of unambiguous cases while routing all ambiguous cases to human review rather than making a potentially wrong automatic decision.

  • Stage 1 — Deterministic matching Established: Match on normalized email address (lowercased, alias-expanded) across systems. If Slack user email matches GitHub verified email, they resolve with 1.0 confidence and are auto-merged. This covers >80% of person-entity resolution in most organizations. Project and feature entities are matched on external identifier fields (e.g., Jira ticket keys embedded in GitHub commit messages via standard convention).
  • Stage 2 — Probabilistic matching Judgment: Where email is absent or inconsistent, match on display name similarity (normalized Levenshtein ≤ 0.15 threshold), username pattern heuristics (first initial + last name patterns), and temporal co-occurrence of activity across systems. Matches with confidence score < 0.85 are flagged as candidate merges and routed to the Human Review Queue — never auto-resolved.
  • Human Review Queue: A lightweight UI surfaces candidate merges for a designated data steward to confirm or reject. Confirmed merges write to the Entity Resolution Index and trigger a graph update. Rejected candidates are stored as non-matches to prevent the system from re-proposing them. All decisions are logged to the Audit Log with the steward's identity and timestamp.
  • Merge reversal: All merges must be reversible. The Entity Resolution Index stores the full merge history, and a reversal operation replays affected events through the pipeline with the corrected entity mapping. This is expensive but must be possible. Judgment — some architectures treat ER decisions as permanent; this design treats them as revisable, accepting the operational cost for the safety benefit.
Rejected Alternative: Fully automated LLM-based entity resolution for all cases. Rejected because LLM-based ER has non-deterministic behavior and can confidently merge distinct entities (two people named "Alex Chen" in different teams, for example). For a system where downstream answers, access control decisions, and misalignment alerts all depend on entity identity, ER errors must be bounded and human-reversible. Fully automated ER is appropriate only where the cost of occasional incorrect merges is low — not in an enterprise intelligence system.
Failure Mode That Worries Me Most: Two real individuals with similar names and email patterns are auto-merged. All queries about either person return a confused blend of their work histories. Beyond being a data quality error, this is a potential privacy violation if one employee's work becomes visible through queries targeting the other. The conservative confidence threshold (0.85) is the primary mitigation. Secondary mitigation: the Human Review Queue drains quickly when staffed — a data steward reviewing 20 candidates per week is sufficient for most organizations. The failure mode is manageable; the critical requirement is that the threshold not be relaxed in production to "reduce operational burden."
4.3Enrichment & Classification Pipeline
→ R1, R3

Why it exists: Normalized events carry structural identity but lack semantic meaning. A Jira ticket status change does not carry a label saying "this is evidence of Feature X drifting from Requirement Y." The Enrichment Pipeline adds semantic content to raw signals: topic classification, implicit cross-reference extraction, misalignment signal scoring, and embedding generation for vector storage. This is the step that transforms raw organizational events into intelligence-bearing artifacts.

Enters

Normalized, entity-resolved events from the ER Pipeline (§4.2).

Leaves

Graph mutations (new/updated nodes and edges) to the Knowledge Graph (§3.2). Chunked, embedded document payloads to the Vector Store (§3.3). PII-scrubbed before either output.

Neighbors

Receives from §4.2. Writes to §3.2 and §3.3. Classification model performance monitored by §7. PII scrubbing outcomes logged to §6.3 (Audit Log).

Enrichment steps applied in sequence:

  1. PII scrubbing: Regex + NER-based redaction of personal data not required for organizational intelligence: phone numbers, personal addresses, financial account numbers. Applied before any downstream storage. Established: Microsoft Presidio (open-source) is the recommended tool. A scrubbing audit log records which fields were redacted.
  2. Topic classification: Lightweight multi-label classifier mapping content to an org-defined topic taxonomy (e.g., "authentication," "payments," "onboarding," "infrastructure"). The taxonomy is org-specific and requires initial seeding by a domain expert; the model is fine-tuned on org content. Judgment — the taxonomy granularity is a significant design decision that must be made with domain stakeholders.
  3. Cross-reference extraction: LLM-assisted extraction of implicit references to other organizational entities in free-text content. "The auth issue from last sprint" → candidate reference to Feature #812. References stored as low-confidence edges in the Knowledge Graph until confirmed or expired. This step runs asynchronously in a batch window, not in the real-time path.
  4. Embedding generation: Documents are chunked (512 tokens, 64-token overlap) and embedded using a fine-tuned sentence embedding model. Each chunk stored in the Vector Store with access control tags inherited from the source document's access scope.
  5. Misalignment signal scoring: Heuristic rules flagging potential R3 signals: feature tickets marked "blocked" for >5 days with no linked Decision explaining the block; commits touching scope beyond the linked Feature definition; customer support tickets referencing features with no linked Requirement. These are candidates for MonitorAgent evaluation, not direct alerts. Judgment — the specific heuristics are tunable and should be calibrated against org-specific patterns.
Failure Mode: LLM-based cross-reference extraction introduces spurious edges into the Knowledge Graph. A message saying "we need to fix this like the payment issue from Q2" creates a false reference to Incident #4412 when the author meant a different incident entirely. False positive edges cause misleading answers to relational queries. Mitigation: all LLM-extracted edges carry a confidence score below 0.7 and are excluded from direct answers; they are used only as retrieval hints, and are presented with explicit uncertainty caveats when surfaced.
Part 1 of 2  ·  AI OS for Companies — Principal Reference Architecture  ·  Continue to Part 2 for Reasoning & Agents, Security & Governance, Observability, Weakness Analysis, and Assumption Log.
arguments.title: AI OS — Reference Architecture Part 1: Responsibilities, Overview, Memory & Ingestion

ai_os_arch_part1.title: AI OS — Reference Architecture Part 1: Responsibilities, Overview, Memory & Ingestion
ai_os_arch_part1.description: Part 1 of 2. Covers: the 5 fundamental responsibilities, system overview with traceability map, all Memory & Knowledge subsystems (3 stores), and all Ingestion & Processing subsystems (3 pipelines) — with alternatives rejected, failure modes, and I/O contracts for each.
ai_os_arch_part1.content:
Principal Reference Architecture  ·  Part 1 of 2  ·  Sections 1–4

AI Operating System for Companies

Responsibility-first reference architecture. This part covers the five fundamental responsibilities, full system overview with traceability map, the three memory & knowledge stores, and the three ingestion & processing pipelines. Every subsystem includes: why it exists, I/O contracts, alternatives rejected, and the failure mode that poses the greatest concern.

Building on: Research report (Parts 1–2). Problem, barriers, and AI foundations not re-derived here. Scope: 500–10,000 employees, 10–60 integrated source systems Legend: Established = well-proven  Judgment = design call  High Risk = extra scrutiny  Open Problem = no settled solution
Part 1 Contents
  1. Five Fundamental Responsibilities
  2. System Overview & Traceability Map
  3. Memory & Knowledge Representation
    • 3.1 Temporal Event Ledger
    • 3.2 Semantic Knowledge Graph (highest risk in this half)
    • 3.3 Vector Retrieval Store
  4. Ingestion & Processing
    • 4.1 Connector Fleet
    • 4.2 Normalization & Entity Resolution Pipeline (high risk)
    • 4.3 Enrichment & Classification Pipeline

Part 2 covers: Reasoning & Agents (deep treatment), Security & Governance (deep treatment), Observability & Evaluation, Infrastructure, Honest Weakness Analysis, and the full Assumption Log.

Section 1

Five Fundamental Responsibilities

These are not features or capabilities. They are the irreducible obligations the system must fulfill for the architectural premise to hold. Every subsystem in this document traces to at least one. If a proposed component cannot be traced here, it is cut. This traceability discipline is the primary mechanism for preventing scope creep in a domain where "just add one more AI feature" pressure is constant.

R1

Capture & Preserve Organizational Memory

Continuously ingest, normalize, and durably store the explicit signals an organization produces — decisions, conversations, work artifacts, operational events — in a form that can be retrieved and reasoned over indefinitely, with full provenance.

R2

Answer Organizational Questions Accurately

Respond to natural-language queries about organizational state — past, present, inferred — with answers that are accurate, cited, calibrated for uncertainty, and explicitly bounded by what the system actually knows vs. what it is inferring.

R3

Detect & Surface Misalignment Proactively

Continuously monitor the relationship between stated intent (OKRs, specs, decisions) and observed execution (commits, deployments, customer signals) and proactively alert when material divergence is detected — without waiting to be asked.

R4

Enforce Information Access Rights

Guarantee that no actor — human or AI agent — accesses information, generates answers, or triggers actions beyond explicitly granted authority. The system must be no less safe than the underlying source systems individually.

R5

Be Continuously Self-Auditable

Provide sufficient internal observability that any stakeholder can determine at any time: what the system knows, how it arrived at any conclusion, whether knowledge is stale, and whether any component is degrading silently without detection.

Responsibility Deliberately Excluded — and Why A sixth responsibility — "Execute autonomous actions on behalf of users" — was considered and excluded from this version of the architecture. Autonomous action crosses a safety threshold requiring independent safety architecture and organizational trust maturity that cannot be assumed in a first-generation deployment. The reasoning layer (§5, Part 2) supports supervised action preparation — generating draft specs, flagging drift, proposing ticket assignments — but stops short of autonomous execution. This is a Judgment call; organizations with higher trust maturity can extend to R6 as a Phase 2 scope addition with a dedicated safety review.
Section 2

System Overview & Traceability Map

Section 3

Memory & Knowledge Representation

Three distinct stores are required because no single storage technology can optimally serve all access patterns simultaneously. This separation is Established polyglot persistence. The specific assignment of organizational memory types to storage technologies is Judgment.

3.1Temporal Event Ledger
→ R1

Why it exists: The system needs an immutable, append-only record of every organizational event in its original, source-attributed form. This is the archival ground truth from which all other stores are derived and to which they can be reconciled when errors occur. Without this, there is no recovery path when the Knowledge Graph or Vector Store are corrupted or when entity resolution decisions need to be revised retroactively.

Enters

Normalized events from the Ingestion layer. Each event carries: source_system, event_type, actor_id (pre-resolution), timestamp, raw_payload, schema_version, idempotency_key.

Leaves

Event replay streams to Knowledge Graph and Vector Store during initial build, schema migration, or error recovery. Provenance records to Audit Log (§6.3).

Neighbors

Receives from Normalization Pipeline (§4.2). Feeds Knowledge Graph (§3.2), Vector Store (§3.3) via replay consumer. Read by Audit Log (§6.3). Monitored by §7.

Technology: Append-only object storage (S3-compatible) with Parquet partitioned by source and date for analytical replay, plus a streaming log (Kafka with long retention) for real-time downstream consumption. Established — this is the Lambda Architecture's batch layer applied to organizational events.

Rejected Alternative: Using the Knowledge Graph as the store of record. Rejected because graphs are mutable by construction — entity resolution merges and relationship updates rewrite history. An immutable ledger is required for audits and for rebuilding downstream stores from scratch when errors accumulate. The ledger-as-source-of-truth pattern is Established (event sourcing, Kleppmann 2017).
Failure Mode That Worries Me Most: Silent event loss during webhook ingestion at high volume. If a source system sends an event burst and the connector drops events without detection — no idempotency key check, no dead-letter queue alert — the ledger has permanent gaps that downstream stores will silently reflect. Gap detection (event sequence monitoring per source) must be built into the ingestion layer as a first-class concern, not deferred to monitoring.
3.2Semantic Knowledge Graph High Risk
→ R1, R2, R3

Why it exists: The vector store enables semantic similarity lookup but cannot answer relational or structural questions: "Which engineers last modified the payment module, and were any of them involved in Tuesday's incident?" Answering this requires traversal over a typed, attributed graph where entities and their relationships are first-class citizens. The Knowledge Graph is the relational reasoning substrate — and the primary determinant of system-wide answer quality. Its correctness is the single most important data quality concern in the entire architecture.

Enters

Entity and relationship mutations from the Enrichment Pipeline (§4.3). Entity resolution decisions from §4.2. Human corrections from Audit Log (§6.3).

Leaves

Subgraphs and traversal results to Reasoning agents (§5). Misalignment signals (diverges_from edges) to MonitorAgent. Entity context to Vector Store for metadata filtering.

Neighbors

Receives from §4.2, §4.3. Serves all §5 agents. All reads gated by Access Control (§6.1). Staleness signals published to Observability (§7).

Core Ontology — Node & Edge Types

NODE TYPES (with key attributes): Person {id, canonical_email, display_names[], roles[], team_ids[], source_ids{}} Team {id, name, parent_team_id, area} Project {id, name, status, owner_id, timeline{start,end}} Feature {id, title, status, priority, spec_url, linked_requirements[]} Requirement {id, text, source_type, customer_segment, priority} Decision {id, summary, rationale, alternatives_rejected[], made_by[], made_at, superseded_by} Incident {id, severity, status, timeline[], root_cause, affected_features[]} Codeunit {id, path, language, last_modified_by, linked_features[]} Message {id, channel_type, author_id, timestamp, summary, source_system} EDGE TYPES (directed, typed, timestamped, confidence-scored): Person --[authored]--> Codeunit | Decision | Feature | Message | Requirement Feature --[implements]--> Requirement Feature --[depends_on]--> Feature Decision --[constrains]--> Feature | Project Incident --[affected]--> Feature | Codeunit Message --[references]--> Feature | Decision | Incident | Requirement | Person Person --[member_of]--> Team (with valid_from, valid_until for org change tracking) Feature --[diverges_from]--> Requirement | Decision ← PRIMARY R3 signal edge KEY DESIGN DECISIONS: All edges carry: {created_at, source_event_id, confidence: 0.0–1.0, created_by: pipeline|human} Edges with confidence < 0.7 are stored but excluded from answers unless explicitly requested. The diverges_from edge is created by MonitorAgent, not by the pipeline — it is an inference, not a direct event signal. This distinction is surfaced in all answers citing it.

Graph Construction & Maintenance

The graph is not manually curated. It is maintained by four automated processes running at different cadences:

  • Real-time event-driven updates: Every ingested event triggers graph mutations via the Enrichment Pipeline. A GitHub commit → authored edge; a new Jira ticket → Feature node; a PagerDuty resolution → Incident node closure + affected edges. Latency target: <60 seconds from source event to graph update.
  • Scheduled LLM-assisted edge inference: A batch job (hourly) runs Message nodes through an LLM extraction prompt to surface implicit cross-references ("the auth issue last sprint" → link to Feature #812). Results stored as low-confidence edges pending confirmation. Open Problem — precision/recall on implicit organizational references is not yet reliably production-grade at scale.
  • Human correction ingestion: The Audit Log (§6.3) surfaces a correction UI for erroneous nodes or edges. Human corrections propagate to the graph as authoritative overrides with provenance and are reflected in the Temporal Event Ledger as correction events.
  • Scheduled staleness marking: A daily job marks nodes stale when their last-updated timestamp exceeds a configurable threshold (default: 30 days for Decisions, 7 days for Feature status). Stale nodes are not deleted — they are flagged, and any answer drawing on them is uncertainty-qualified automatically.
Rejected Alternative A: Using a pure vector store with LLM-based relational reasoning over retrieved text chunks. Rejected because relational questions (graph traversal, typed path-finding, aggregation over relationship types) are not reliably answerable by LLM reasoning over flat retrieval. The error rate on multi-hop relational questions without a graph structure is unacceptably high for an enterprise trust model. Established: knowledge graphs for relational reasoning are proven at Google (KG), LinkedIn (Economic Graph), Microsoft (MSR KG).
Rejected Alternative B: A single mega-graph storing all organizational data in a commercial knowledge graph platform (e.g., Stardog, Cambridge Semantics). Rejected because ontology lock-in to a proprietary schema makes future schema evolution costly. Preferred technology: Neo4j (open-source core) or Amazon Neptune with a custom ontology layer. Judgment.
Failure Mode That Worries Me Most: Entity resolution errors compounding silently. If "Alice Johnson" in Slack and "ajohnson" in GitHub are never resolved to the same Person node, all authored edges for this individual are split. Queries about "Alice's work" return partial results that look complete. The critical property: this failure produces no error signal — the system returns an answer with apparent confidence. This is the highest-risk silent failure mode in the entire system. Mitigation strategy detailed in §4.2.
3.3Vector Retrieval Store
→ R1, R2

Why it exists: Full-text and semantic similarity retrieval over unstructured organizational content — meeting transcripts, Slack threads, PR descriptions, support tickets, design docs — cannot be satisfied by graph traversal. The Vector Store handles "find me content semantically related to this concept" at sub-second latency across millions of documents. It is the primary retrieval substrate for the QueryAgent's RAG pipeline.

Enters

Chunked, embedded documents from the Enrichment Pipeline. Each chunk carries: source_id, entity_refs[], access_control_tags[], timestamp, embedding_vector, chunk_type, staleness_flag.

Leaves

Top-K semantically similar chunks with full metadata to the Reasoning Layer. Access control tags enforced before returning results to the requesting agent's identity context.

Neighbors

Receives from §4.3. Queried by QueryAgent and SynthesisAgent (§5). Access control enforced by §6.1 at query time. Monitored by §7 for retrieval quality degradation.

Retrieval strategy: Hybrid — dense vector search (ANN over embeddings) + sparse BM25/keyword search, fused via Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF). Established — hybrid retrieval consistently outperforms either approach alone across organizational content benchmarks. Technology: Weaviate or Qdrant for dense; Elasticsearch for sparse; RRF fusion in the QueryAgent retrieval planner.

Rejected Alternative: A single dense-only vector database. Rejected because BM25 outperforms dense search for exact-match queries on organizational identifiers: ticket numbers, feature names, commit hashes, employee names. Dense-only systems fail on queries like "show me everything about PROJ-4412." Hybrid is slightly more complex but the retrieval quality improvement is not marginal — it is substantial for organizational content. Established.
Failure Mode That Worries Me Most: Embedding model vocabulary staleness. When the organization adopts new terminology — a new product name, strategic initiative, or technical concept — that the embedding model was not trained on, semantic search silently fails to retrieve relevant documents. No error is raised; results are just irrelevant. Mitigation: hybrid retrieval with BM25 as fallback for terms absent from embedding vocabulary; organization-specific vocabulary fine-tuning for embedding models on a quarterly cadence. This is Open Problem for continuous vocabulary evolution.
Section 4

Ingestion & Processing

4.1Connector Fleet
→ R1

Why it exists: Each source system has a different API contract, rate limit, authentication model, event schema, and retry behavior. The Connector Fleet isolates this heterogeneity behind a uniform event stream interface. Without this isolation, every downstream processing stage would embed source-specific logic, creating n×m coupling complexity that makes adding new integrations prohibitively expensive.

Enters

Raw API responses, webhook payloads, or CDC event streams. Authenticated per-source via OAuth 2.0, API key, or service account credentials stored in a secrets manager (not in the connector config).

Leaves

Raw events written to the message broker (Kafka topics, one per source-system type) with envelope: {source, event_type, raw_payload, received_at, idempotency_key, schema_version}.

Neighbors

Pulls/receives from source systems. Pushes to message broker. Connector health metrics published to Observability (§7). Credential rotation managed externally via secrets manager.

Key implementation decisions: Connectors are isolated processes — a failing Salesforce connector does not affect GitHub ingestion. Established: process isolation for connector fleets is standard in data platform engineering. Idempotency keys prevent duplicate processing on retries. A dead-letter queue (DLQ) captures events failing schema validation. Rate-limit budgets are managed per-connector with token-bucket throttling and exponential backoff. All secrets are injected at runtime, never stored in connector configuration files.

Rejected Alternative: Commercial iPaaS (Zapier, Workato, Boomi). Rejected because iPaaS tools are optimized for action orchestration, not high-volume observable event streaming. They also introduce a vendor-controlled data path that creates compliance complexity (data leaves the org's control boundary to route through a third party). Airbyte (open-source) is preferred for batch connectors; lightweight custom webhook receivers for real-time connectors. Judgment — iPaaS may be appropriate for very early-stage deployments where speed of connector coverage outweighs data governance concerns.
Failure Mode: Source system API schema changes silently break connectors, causing events to fail validation and accumulate in the DLQ without detection. Mitigation: schema validation on every event at connector egress with immediate alerting when validation failure rate exceeds 1% over any 15-minute window.
4.2Normalization & Entity Resolution Pipeline High Risk
→ R1

Why it exists: Raw events from different systems use incompatible schemas and identity namespaces. "user_id: sf_0048X" in Salesforce and "login: ajohnson" in GitHub may be the same person — this must be resolved before knowledge is stored, or the Knowledge Graph fragments into disconnected islands making cross-tool reasoning impossible. This pipeline is the semantic foundation of the entire system. Its errors are the most damaging and the hardest to detect.

Enters

Raw events from the Connector Fleet via message broker. Each carries source-system identity fields: email addresses, usernames, user IDs, display names, project identifiers.

Leaves

Normalized events with all source-local identifiers replaced by canonical entity IDs. Written to the Temporal Event Ledger (§3.1) and forwarded downstream. Also writes to the Entity Resolution Index (a separate mapping store).

Neighbors

Receives from §4.1. Writes to §3.1 (Ledger). Maintains the Entity Resolution Index. Sends candidate merges to the Human Review Queue. Monitored by §7 for resolution confidence distribution.

Entity Resolution Strategy

ER is solved in two stages, applied in sequence. The goal is to maximize auto-resolution of unambiguous cases while routing all ambiguous cases to human review rather than making a potentially wrong automatic decision.

  • Stage 1 — Deterministic matching Established: Match on normalized email address (lowercased, alias-expanded) across systems. If Slack user email matches GitHub verified email, they resolve with 1.0 confidence and are auto-merged. This covers >80% of person-entity resolution in most organizations. Project and feature entities are matched on external identifier fields (e.g., Jira ticket keys embedded in GitHub commit messages via standard convention).
  • Stage 2 — Probabilistic matching Judgment: Where email is absent or inconsistent, match on display name similarity (normalized Levenshtein ≤ 0.15 threshold), username pattern heuristics (first initial + last name patterns), and temporal co-occurrence of activity across systems. Matches with confidence score < 0.85 are flagged as candidate merges and routed to the Human Review Queue — never auto-resolved.
  • Human Review Queue: A lightweight UI surfaces candidate merges for a designated data steward to confirm or reject. Confirmed merges write to the Entity Resolution Index and trigger a graph update. Rejected candidates are stored as non-matches to prevent the system from re-proposing them. All decisions are logged to the Audit Log with the steward's identity and timestamp.
  • Merge reversal: All merges must be reversible. The Entity Resolution Index stores the full merge history, and a reversal operation replays affected events through the pipeline with the corrected entity mapping. This is expensive but must be possible. Judgment — some architectures treat ER decisions as permanent; this design treats them as revisable, accepting the operational cost for the safety benefit.
Rejected Alternative: Fully automated LLM-based entity resolution for all cases. Rejected because LLM-based ER has non-deterministic behavior and can confidently merge distinct entities (two people named "Alex Chen" in different teams, for example). For a system where downstream answers, access control decisions, and misalignment alerts all depend on entity identity, ER errors must be bounded and human-reversible. Fully automated ER is appropriate only where the cost of occasional incorrect merges is low — not in an enterprise intelligence system.
Failure Mode That Worries Me Most: Two real individuals with similar names and email patterns are auto-merged. All queries about either person return a confused blend of their work histories. Beyond being a data quality error, this is a potential privacy violation if one employee's work becomes visible through queries targeting the other. The conservative confidence threshold (0.85) is the primary mitigation. Secondary mitigation: the Human Review Queue drains quickly when staffed — a data steward reviewing 20 candidates per week is sufficient for most organizations. The failure mode is manageable; the critical requirement is that the threshold not be relaxed in production to "reduce operational burden."
4.3Enrichment & Classification Pipeline
→ R1, R3

Why it exists: Normalized events carry structural identity but lack semantic meaning. A Jira ticket status change does not carry a label saying "this is evidence of Feature X drifting from Requirement Y." The Enrichment Pipeline adds semantic content to raw signals: topic classification, implicit cross-reference extraction, misalignment signal scoring, and embedding generation for vector storage. This is the step that transforms raw organizational events into intelligence-bearing artifacts.

Enters

Normalized, entity-resolved events from the ER Pipeline (§4.2).

Leaves

Graph mutations (new/updated nodes and edges) to the Knowledge Graph (§3.2). Chunked, embedded document payloads to the Vector Store (§3.3). PII-scrubbed before either output.

Neighbors

Receives from §4.2. Writes to §3.2 and §3.3. Classification model performance monitored by §7. PII scrubbing outcomes logged to §6.3 (Audit Log).

Enrichment steps applied in sequence:

  1. PII scrubbing: Regex + NER-based redaction of personal data not required for organizational intelligence: phone numbers, personal addresses, financial account numbers. Applied before any downstream storage. Established: Microsoft Presidio (open-source) is the recommended tool. A scrubbing audit log records which fields were redacted.
  2. Topic classification: Lightweight multi-label classifier mapping content to an org-defined topic taxonomy (e.g., "authentication," "payments," "onboarding," "infrastructure"). The taxonomy is org-specific and requires initial seeding by a domain expert; the model is fine-tuned on org content. Judgment — the taxonomy granularity is a significant design decision that must be made with domain stakeholders.
  3. Cross-reference extraction: LLM-assisted extraction of implicit references to other organizational entities in free-text content. "The auth issue from last sprint" → candidate reference to Feature #812. References stored as low-confidence edges in the Knowledge Graph until confirmed or expired. This step runs asynchronously in a batch window, not in the real-time path.
  4. Embedding generation: Documents are chunked (512 tokens, 64-token overlap) and embedded using a fine-tuned sentence embedding model. Each chunk stored in the Vector Store with access control tags inherited from the source document's access scope.
  5. Misalignment signal scoring: Heuristic rules flagging potential R3 signals: feature tickets marked "blocked" for >5 days with no linked Decision explaining the block; commits touching scope beyond the linked Feature definition; customer support tickets referencing features with no linked Requirement. These are candidates for MonitorAgent evaluation, not direct alerts. Judgment — the specific heuristics are tunable and should be calibrated against org-specific patterns.
Failure Mode: LLM-based cross-reference extraction introduces spurious edges into the Knowledge Graph. A message saying "we need to fix this like the payment issue from Q2" creates a false reference to Incident #4412 when the author meant a different incident entirely. False positive edges cause misleading answers to relational queries. Mitigation: all LLM-extracted edges carry a confidence score below 0.7 and are excluded from direct answers; they are used only as retrieval hints, and are presented with explicit uncertainty caveats when surfaced.
Part 1 of 2  ·  AI OS for Companies — Principal Reference Architecture  ·  Continue to Part 2 for Reasoning & Agents, Security & Governance, Observability, Weakness Analysis, and Assumption Log.

arguments.html:
Principal Reference Architecture  ·  Part 2 of 2  ·  Sections 5–10

AI Operating System for Companies

Continues from Part 1. Deep treatment of the two highest-risk subsystems: Reasoning & Agents (§5) and Security & Governance (§6). Followed by Observability, Infrastructure, a plain-language weakness analysis, and the full assumption log.

Legend: Established = well-proven  Judgment = design call  High Risk = extra scrutiny  Open Problem = no settled solution
Part 2 Contents
  1. Reasoning & Agents — Deep Treatment
    • 5.1 Four Agent Classes, Derived from Responsibilities
    • 5.2 Inter-Agent Communication Protocol
    • 5.3 Human Supervision Architecture
    • 5.4 Faithfulness Verification Subsystem
  2. Security & Governance — Deep Treatment
    • 6.1 Identity-Bound Access Control
    • 6.2 Conflict & Contradiction Detection
    • 6.3 Audit & Correction Log
  3. Observability & Evaluation
  4. Infrastructure & Deployment
  5. Honest Weakness Analysis
  6. Explicit Assumption Log
Section 5 — Deep Treatment (Highest-Risk Subsystem)

Reasoning & Agents

This is the highest-risk subsystem in the architecture. It is where answers are generated, where hallucination can occur, where agent errors compound, and where user trust is either established or permanently lost. It is treated at disproportionate depth relative to its nominal position in the stack.

The design question asked first: how many distinct agent classes does this system truly need? This is answered by deriving agent classes from the system's responsibilities — not from a desire to demonstrate agentic sophistication.

5.1 Four Agent Classes — Derived from Responsibilities

Each agent class exists because it handles a cognitively distinct task that cannot be collapsed into another without creating an agent with conflicting objectives or untestable behavior. Judgment: a monolithic "reasoning service" is the simpler alternative. The four-agent split is justified by the quality attribute of verifiability — each agent can be tested, benchmarked, and replaced independently.

Agent ClassResp.InputsOutputsCannot Be Merged With
QueryAgent R2 User query + session identity context. Executes hybrid retrieval against Vector Store and Knowledge Graph. Decomposes complex queries into sub-queries. Draft answer + retrieved source list + confidence estimate. Passed to VerificationAgent before delivery. May spawn a SynthesisAgent task for multi-source complexity. MonitorAgent — mixing reactive answering with proactive monitoring creates conflicting scheduling priorities and makes behavioral testing significantly harder.
MonitorAgent R3 Scheduled trigger (every N minutes) + Knowledge Graph change feed. Evaluates diverges_from edge candidates and stale-node signals against configurable misalignment rules. Typed misalignment alerts: {scope_drift, delivery_delay, contradiction_detected, orphaned_requirement, stale_decision}. Written to alert queue, delivered via Interface Layer. QueryAgent — proactive monitoring requires persistent state, scheduled execution, and a different latency budget (minutes vs. seconds). Merging them creates resource contention and unclear restart semantics.
SynthesisAgent R2, R3 Synthesis task spec from QueryAgent or MonitorAgent: {sources[], output_format, scope_constraints, requester_identity}. Never initiates work independently. Structured artifacts with full source attribution: decision summaries, spec drafts, incident timelines, cross-team briefings, misalignment reports. Always forwarded to VerificationAgent. VerificationAgent — separating synthesis from verification ensures the verifier has no stake in the output it is checking. Merging them is the most dangerous possible collapse.
VerificationAgent R2, R5 Draft answer or synthesis artifact from QueryAgent/SynthesisAgent + the exact retrieved sources used to produce it. No other inputs — it must work only from what the producing agent used. Verdict: {PASS / UNCERTAIN / FAIL} with per-claim annotation. PASS → deliver. UNCERTAIN → deliver with caveat. FAIL → block, log to Audit Log, route to human reviewer. Any other agent. The VerificationAgent's independence is its entire value. It must share no mutable state with the agent whose output it is checking. This is non-negotiable.
On Not Having More Agents "CoordinatorAgent," "PlannerAgent," "MemoryAgent," and "RouterAgent" were each considered. Every responsibility they would hold is either already covered by the four agents above or belongs in infrastructure (routing is a message broker concern). Adding agents increases coordination surface, makes debugging harder, and multiplies failure modes. Design bias: fewer agents with precisely bounded responsibilities.

5.2 Inter-Agent Communication Protocol

Agents do not call each other directly. All inter-agent communication passes through the shared task queue (backed by the same message broker used in ingestion, a separate topic namespace). Direct coupling creates circular dependency risks and prevents independent scaling. Established: message-passing over shared queues is standard for multi-agent coordination in production systems.

TASK ENVELOPE SCHEMA: { "task_id": "uuid-v4", "task_type": "QUERY | SYNTHESIS | VERIFY | MONITOR_CHECK", "originator": "agent_class_id | user_id", "priority": 1-5, "deadline_ms": 5000, "audit_required": true, "payload": { ...task-type-specific fields... }, "context": { "user_identity": "canonical_user_id", "access_scopes": ["team:engineering", "project:auth-service"], "session_id": "uuid-v4", "parent_task_id": "uuid-v4 | null" ← enables full chain tracing } } ROUTING FLOW: User submits query │ ▼ Task{QUERY} → queue → QueryAgent │ ├── [simple, single-source] ──────────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ └── [complex, multi-source] → Task{SYNTHESIS} → SynthesisAgent │ │ │ └──── draft produced ────────┤ │ Task{VERIFY} → VerificationAgent │ ┌──────────────────────────┤ │ │ PASS/UNCERTAIN FAIL │ │ Interface Layer Audit Log + → User Human Reviewer Queue MonitorAgent runs on schedule: │ ├── evaluates diverges_from candidates └── [misalignment detected] → Task{SYNTHESIS} (misalignment report) → Task{VERIFY} → Alert Queue → Interface Layer → Subscribers

Principal Reference Architecture  ·  Part 2 of 2  ·  Sections 5–10

AI Operating System for Companies

Deep treatment of the two highest-risk subsystems (Reasoning & Agents, Security & Governance), followed by Observability, Infrastructure, a plain-language weakness analysis, and the full assumption log.

Legend: Established = proven pattern  Judgment = design call  High Risk = scrutinize  Open Problem = no settled solution
Part 2 Contents
  1. Reasoning & Agents — Deep Treatment
    • 5.1 Four Agent Classes (derived from responsibilities)
    • 5.2 Inter-Agent Communication Protocol
    • 5.3 Human Supervision Architecture
    • 5.4 Faithfulness Verification Subsystem
  2. Security & Governance — Deep Treatment
    • 6.1 Identity-Bound Access Control
    • 6.2 Conflict & Contradiction Detection
    • 6.3 Audit & Correction Log
  3. Observability & Evaluation
  4. Infrastructure & Deployment

Section 5 — Deep Treatment (Highest-Risk Subsystem)

Reasoning & Agents

This is where answers are generated, hallucination can occur, agent errors compound, and user trust is earned or permanently destroyed. It receives disproportionate depth. The design question asked first: exactly how many distinct agent classes does this system truly need? Derived from responsibilities, not from enthusiasm for agentic complexity.

5.1 Four Agent Classes — Derived from Responsibilities

Each class handles a cognitively distinct task with a distinct objective. Collapsing any two creates an agent with conflicting objectives or untestable behavior. Judgment: a monolithic reasoning service is the simpler alternative; the four-agent split is justified by the verifiability quality attribute.

AgentResp.InputsOutputsCannot Merge With
QueryAgent R2 User query + session identity. Runs hybrid retrieval (Vector Store + Knowledge Graph). Decomposes complex queries into sub-queries. Draft answer + source list + confidence estimate → forwarded to VerificationAgent. Complex queries spawn a SynthesisAgent task. MonitorAgent — conflicting scheduling priorities; reactive vs. proactive objectives cannot share a single behavioral spec.
MonitorAgent R3 Scheduled trigger + Knowledge Graph change feed. Evaluates diverges_from edge candidates against configurable misalignment rules. Typed alerts: {scope_drift, delivery_delay, contradiction_detected, orphaned_requirement, stale_decision}. Routes to alert queue. QueryAgent — different latency budget (minutes vs. seconds), requires persistent state across runs, different restart semantics.
SynthesisAgent R2, R3 Synthesis task spec from QueryAgent or MonitorAgent: sources[], output_format, scope_constraints, requester_identity. Never self-initiates. Structured artifacts with full source attribution: decision summaries, spec drafts, incident timelines, cross-team briefings. Always → VerificationAgent. VerificationAgent — the verifier must have no stake in the output it checks. Merging synthesis and verification is the single most dangerous possible collapse in this architecture.

arguments.html:
Reference Architecture  ·  Part 3 of 3  ·  Critical Sections

AI OS — Human Supervision, Governance, Weaknesses & Assumptions

Covers: human supervision architecture, faithfulness verification, identity-bound access control, conflict detection, audit log, observability, infrastructure minimums, four plainly-stated weaknesses, and the complete twelve-item assumption log.

Section 5.3

Human Supervision Architecture

Agents operating in an enterprise environment require structured human oversight. The supervision model defines three intervention points, each corresponding to a different failure type and urgency level. The design is Judgment — the specific thresholds are configurable; the three-tier structure is a principled design choice.

TIER 1 — INLINE REVIEW (synchronous, human-in-the-loop) Triggered by: VerificationAgent FAIL verdict Latency impact: user sees "pending human review" status (max 4h SLA) Human action: approve revised answer, reject query, or escalate Volume expectation: < 2% of all queries in a mature deployment Failure risk: human reviewer fatigue if FAIL rate is not bounded TIER 2 — ASYNC REVIEW (asynchronous, human-on-the-loop) Triggered by: VerificationAgent UNCERTAIN verdict, MonitorAgent alerts above severity threshold Latency impact: none — answers/alerts delivered with explicit uncertainty caveat Human action: confirm or refute within 24h; decision written to Audit Log Volume expectation: 5–15% of queries in the first 90 days; should decline as model improves Failure risk: alert fatigue causing reviewers to rubber-stamp without reading TIER 3 — RETROSPECTIVE AUDIT (weekly/monthly) Triggered by: scheduled, or by drift detected in Observability layer (§7) Latency impact: none Human action: review a random sample of PASS verdicts, correct any silent errors found Volume expectation: 2–5% sample of total query volume Failure risk: retrospective audits treated as box-checking; errors found but not fed back into the model improvement loop

data.content: The HTML limit keeps truncating the larger sections. Let me deliver the critical remaining content — the **Weakness Analysis** and **Assumption Log** — as clean, standalone artifacts since these are the most analytically important outputs:

arguments.description: The four honest weaknesses of the AI OS architecture stated plainly (no glossing), plus the complete 12-item explicit assumption log with type labels and failure conditions for each assumption.
arguments.html:
Reference Architecture  ·  Critical Analysis Sections

AI OS — Weaknesses, Governance, Observability & Assumption Log

This document covers the security & governance subsystems, the observability layer, the honest weakness analysis (four weaknesses stated plainly), and the complete twelve-item assumption log. These sections are the most analytically important for evaluating whether this architecture should be built as described.

Section 6 — Deep Treatment (Second-Highest-Risk Subsystem)

Security & Governance

Governance failure in an enterprise AI OS does not look like a dramatic breach. It looks like an engineer asking "who approved the decision to remove the rate limiter?" and getting an answer that is correct for the wrong person, or a manager receiving a performance summary that includes data from a confidential HR process they were not authorized to see. These failures erode trust permanently and are typically discovered months after they begin occurring.

6.1 Identity-Bound Access Control

Why it exists: The system aggregates data from dozens of source systems, each with its own access control model. If the intelligence layer ignores these models and serves any knowledge to any authenticated user, it becomes a universal bypass for every access control decision every source system has ever made. R4 is non-negotiable. Established principle: the intelligence layer cannot be less restrictive than any source system it draws from.

PERMISSION INHERITANCE MODEL: Source System Access Policy (GitHub: repo access · Slack: channel membership · Notion: page permissions) │ │ Synchronized at ingestion time via Connector Fleet ▼ Access Tag applied to every stored artifact: { "artifact_id": "...", "access_tags": { "min_scope": "team:payments", ← minimum required scope "restricted": ["project:perf-reviews"], ← explicit deny list "pii": true ← requires PII-access grant } } │ │ Enforced at read time, not at write time ▼ Query arrives with user_identity context: { "user_id": "canonical_user_id", "scopes": ["team:payments", "team:engineering"], "grants": ["pii:own-team-only"] } │ ▼ Access Control Layer evaluates: artifact.access_tags ∩ user.scopes ≠ ∅ AND artifact.restricted ∩ user.id = ∅ AND (artifact.pii = false OR user.grants includes pii) │ [PASS]────────────► included in retrieval context [FAIL]────────────► excluded silently (not disclosed to user) IMPORTANT: Exclusions are not disclosed to the requester. "I don't have information about that" is the correct response, not "That document exists but you can't see it."

arguments.description: Four weaknesses of the AI OS architecture stated plainly — no glossing, no mitigation padding — plus the complete 12-item explicit assumption log with type tags and failure conditions for each assumption.
arguments.html:
Reference Architecture  ·  Critical Analysis  ·  Sections 6–10

AI OS — Governance, Observability, Weaknesses & Assumptions

The governance and observability subsystems, followed by the four weaknesses of this architecture stated plainly without glossing, and the complete twelve-item explicit assumption log with failure conditions for each.

Section 6 — Deep Treatment

Security & Governance

6.1 Identity-Bound Access Control Established Principle

Why it exists: This system aggregates data from dozens of source systems, each with its own access model. An intelligence layer that ignores those models becomes a universal bypass for every access control decision every source system ever made — a single query can surface confidential HR notes, unreleased product roadmaps, and personal compensation data to anyone with a chat interface. R4 is the hardest non-negotiable in the design.

Design principle: Access tags are attached to every stored artifact at ingestion time, inherited from source system permissions synchronized by the Connector Fleet. Access is evaluated at read time, not write time, because permissions change after ingestion. The system must re-evaluate access on every retrieval call — caching permission state longer than 15 minutes is a governance risk.

ACCESS CONTROL FLOW: At Ingestion: Source system permissions → synced by Connector Fleet → Access tag attached to every artifact: { min_scope, restricted_to[], pii_flag, sensitivity_level } At Query Time: User presents identity context: { user_id, scopes[], grants[] } For each retrieved artifact: ALLOW if: artifact.min_scope ⊆ user.scopes AND user.id ∉ artifact.restricted_to AND (artifact.pii = false OR "pii" ∈ user.grants) DENY if: any condition above fails → artifact silently excluded from context → not disclosed to user that it exists CRITICAL RULE: "I don't have information about that" is the correct response to a query about something that exists but is access-denied. Disclosing that a restricted artifact exists is itself a disclosure.

R4 is the hardest non-negotiable in the design.

Design principle: Access tags are attached to every stored artifact at ingestion time, inherited from source system permissions synchronized by the Connector Fleet. Access is evaluated at read time, not write time, because permissions change after ingestion. The system must re-evaluate access on every retrieval call — caching permission state longer than 15 minutes is a governance risk.

ACCESS CONTROL FLOW: At Ingestion: Source system permissions → synced by Connector Fleet → Access tag attached to every artifact: { min_scope, restricted_to[], pii_flag, sensitivity_level } At Query Time: User presents identity context: { user_id, scopes[], grants[] } For each retrieved artifact: ALLOW if: artifact.min_scope ⊆ user.scopes AND user.id ∉ artifact.restricted_to AND (artifact.pii = false OR "pii" ∈ user.grants) DENY if: any condition above fails → artifact silently excluded from context → not disclosed to user that it exists CRITICAL RULE: "I don't have information about that" is the correct response to a query about something that exists but is access-denied. Disclosing that a restricted artifact exists is itself a disclosure.
