ai_os_weakness_assumptions.content:
Reference Architecture  ·  Critical Analysis  ·  Sections 6–10

AI OS — Governance, Observability, Weaknesses & Assumptions

The governance and observability subsystems, followed by the four weaknesses of this architecture stated plainly without glossing, and the complete twelve-item explicit assumption log with failure conditions for each.

Section 6 — Deep Treatment

Security & Governance

6.1 Identity-Bound Access Control Established Principle

Why it exists: This system aggregates data from dozens of source systems, each with its own access model. An intelligence layer that ignores those models becomes a universal bypass for every access control decision every source system ever made — a single query can surface confidential HR notes, unreleased product roadmaps, and personal compensation data to anyone with a chat interface. R4 is the hardest non-negotiable in the design.

Design principle: Access tags are attached to every stored artifact at ingestion time, inherited from source system permissions synchronized by the Connector Fleet. Access is evaluated at read time, not write time, because permissions change after ingestion. The system must re-evaluate access on every retrieval call — caching permission state longer than 15 minutes is a governance risk.

ACCESS CONTROL FLOW: At Ingestion: Source system permissions → synced by Connector Fleet → Access tag attached to every artifact: { min_scope, restricted_to[], pii_flag, sensitivity_level } At Query Time: User presents identity context: { user_id, scopes[], grants[] } For each retrieved artifact: ALLOW if: artifact.min_scope ⊆ user.scopes AND user.id ∉ artifact.restricted_to AND (artifact.pii = false OR "pii" ∈ user.grants) DENY if: any condition above fails → artifact silently excluded from context → not disclosed to user that it exists CRITICAL RULE: "I don't have information about that" is the correct response to a query about something that exists but is access-denied. Disclosing that a restricted artifact exists is itself a disclosure.

data.content: I keep hitting the size limit before reaching the weakness and assumption sections. Let me deliver those two critical sections directly now as clean, focused artifacts — one for weaknesses, one for assumptions:

arguments.description: Four structural weaknesses of the AI OS architecture stated plainly: Entity Resolution Precision, Governance Sync Latency, Reasoning Reliability at Tail, and Organizational Adoption. No glossing over, specific failure modes named.
arguments.html:
Reference Architecture  ·  Weakness Analysis  ·  Section 9

AI OS — Four Structural Weaknesses

The 3–4 quality attributes most likely to be weak for this specific system, with the actual weakness stated plainly. No mitigation is sufficient to eliminate these — they are genuine constraints that any realistic deployment must plan around.

How these were selected: After completing the architecture, I asked: "If I deployed this at a 2,000-person company tomorrow, which four failure modes would I be most afraid to explain to the board six months later?" The answer to that question is this section. These are not theoretical risks — they are the predictable, structurally-embedded weaknesses of a system with these components. A design that claims uniform strength across all quality attributes is not trustworthy.
Weakness 1  ·  Data Quality

Entity Resolution Is Fragile and Silently Fails

The architecture depends on the Semantic Knowledge Graph producing accurate, complete entity linkages across 10–60 heterogeneous source systems. The entity resolution pipeline uses email matching as its primary signal and probabilistic fallback for the rest. In practice, organizations have inconsistent email hygiene: contractors use personal emails, acquired team members retain legacy domains, and service accounts share display names with humans.

The actual weakness: A material fraction of Person entities will be split across multiple nodes, never resolved. Every split entity means all answers about that person's contributions, decisions, and work are silently incomplete. The system returns a confident partial answer. There is no error signal. The user has no way to know they received 60% of the relevant information instead of 100%.
Why mitigation is only partial
The human review queue (§4.2) catches ambiguous merges before they happen, but it cannot catch the case where two entities are never proposed for merging at all because the signals are too dissimilar (different email domains, completely different username styles, no temporal co-occurrence). Those cases are invisible to the ER pipeline and invisible to reviewers. They accumulate silently.
What good looks like vs. what we will likely get
Good: >95% of real-world entity co-references correctly resolved within 72 hours of first co-occurrence. Realistic year-one target: 80–88% for Person entities, lower for Projects and Features (which have less consistent cross-system identity signals). The 12–20% error rate is not evenly distributed — it concentrates in the people and projects that generate the most cross-tool activity, which are also the ones most frequently queried.
Weakness 2  ·  Security

Access Control Sync Creates a Permission Lag Window

The access control model relies on permission tags synchronized from source systems at ingestion time. When someone is removed from a GitHub repository, added to a confidential Slack channel, or promoted to a role with new access scope, that change must propagate through the Connector Fleet to update the access tags on all affected stored artifacts.

The actual weakness: This synchronization is not instantaneous. There is a window — ranging from minutes to hours depending on connector polling intervals and source API rate limits — during which the access tags in the intelligence layer do not reflect the current permission state of the source system. During this window, a recently-removed user can still receive answers drawing on artifacts they should no longer access. A recently-added user cannot yet access what they should.
The harder half of this problem
The "recently-added user can't access yet" case is annoying but safe. The "recently-removed user still has access" case is a genuine security exposure. For ordinary access changes, the lag is low-risk. For terminations for cause, legal holds, or incident-related access restriction, hours of lag is unacceptable. This requires a break-glass mechanism: on-demand permission flush for specific users, callable by IT/Security, that bypasses the normal sync cadence. Without this, the system cannot be used in organizations with strict access control SLAs.
What the architecture does not solve
Unsolved: Retroactive access. If a document was included in an answer given yesterday, and today that document's access was revoked, the answer was already delivered. There is no mechanism to retroactively invalidate delivered answers. This is a known limitation of all RAG-based systems and must be disclosed to legal and compliance stakeholders before deployment.
Weakness 3  ·  Reliability

Reasoning Quality Degrades Badly at the Tail

For common, well-represented query types — "What was the last decision about X?" "Who worked on Y?" "Show me recent incidents related to Z?" — the architecture's RAG pipeline and VerificationAgent produce reliable, cited answers. This is the performance you would observe in a demo or a benchmark.

The actual weakness: For complex multi-hop queries, temporally-anchored questions, and questions that require integrating contradictory information from multiple sources, accuracy degrades non-linearly. "Given the decision made in Q3, the incident that happened last month, and the current sprint status, what is the risk that the payment feature ships with the compliance gap?" requires multi-hop reasoning across four entity types, temporal reasoning, and synthesizing potentially conflicting signals. Current LLMs do this unreliably. The VerificationAgent will catch some failures, but not all — particularly when the retrieved sources are individually accurate but collectively misleading.
The trust calibration problem
The dangerous scenario is not the one where the system obviously fails — it is the one where the system produces a fluent, confident, plausible answer to a complex question that contains a subtle error. In a demo, these answers impress. In production, one high-profile confident error on a consequential question will damage trust far more than ten obvious failures. The uncertainty flag in UNCERTAIN verdicts partially mitigates this, but users habituate to caveats over time and start ignoring them.
Honest target
Realistic: >90% accuracy on simple single-hop queries; 70–80% on multi-hop queries in year one; <60% on complex temporally-anchored multi-entity synthesis. The last category should be presented as "draft for human review" rather than as answers, and this constraint must be communicated to users during onboarding — not discovered through trial and error.
Weakness 4  ·  Adoptability

The System Quality Depends on Organizational Inputs It Cannot Control

The architecture is designed to ingest and reason over organizational artifacts — specs, decisions, tickets, meeting notes, PRs. Its quality is directly proportional to the quality and completeness of those inputs. The system has no mechanism to improve the discipline with which an organization produces them.

The actual weakness: In most organizations, the contextual knowledge layer — the "why" of decisions — is the most valuable and the least consistently captured. Design rationale lives in Slack threads that aren't archived. ADRs are written inconsistently. Spec documents are written once and never updated. Meeting notes are not taken. The intelligence layer will excel at answering questions about the 20% of organizational context that is consistently documented, and will struggle with or fabricate answers about the 80% that is not. Users will encounter this gap quickly, lose confidence in the system, and reduce their usage — a death spiral for adoption.
Why this is an architectural weakness and not just a people problem
The architecture could be designed to make knowledge capture easier — integrating a "capture this decision" affordance directly into Slack, flagging when a significant code change has no linked spec, prompting for decision rationale in PR templates. These affordances reduce the friction of externalization. They do not eliminate it. The fundamental constraint — that tacit knowledge requires human effort to convert to explicit knowledge — cannot be engineered away. Any deployment plan that does not include an organizational change management component alongside the technical deployment is incomplete.
The specific failure mode to watch for
Watch for: The system performs excellently for the first 90 days on historical data accumulated over years. Then new-context quality degrades as the team notices that recent decisions are not being documented into the system. The system's knowledge of "what happened six months ago" remains good; its knowledge of "what was decided last week" degrades. Users begin to say the system is "stuck in the past." This is not a model problem — it is a contribution discipline problem that must be managed organizationally.
AI OS Reference Architecture  ·  Weakness Analysis  ·  Section 9 of 10
arguments.title: AI OS — Four Structural Weaknesses (Stated Plainly)

arguments.description: Complete 12-item explicit assumption log for the AI OS architecture. Each assumption is typed (technical/organizational/economic/governance/AI), given a failure condition, and labeled as established vs. judgment call.
arguments.html:
Reference Architecture  ·  Assumption Log  ·  Section 10

AI OS — Explicit Assumption Log

Every design decision rests on assumptions. Making them explicit prevents the architecture from being applied uncritically to contexts where the assumptions do not hold. Each assumption is typed, given a failure condition, and labeled established or judgment call.

Type: Technical Organizational Economic Governance AI/Model     Basis: Established = well-supported by evidence   Judgment = reasonable but contestable design call
A1TechnicalEstablishedSource systems expose adequate APIs or webhooks

The architecture assumes that all target source systems expose APIs or webhooks with sufficient event coverage, data completeness, and rate limits to support the ingestion pipeline. This is true for the tier-1 systems (GitHub, Slack, Linear, Notion, Salesforce) but becomes less reliable for legacy enterprise systems, on-premises tools, and heavily customized deployments of common platforms.

If this assumption fails: Connector coverage is lower than planned. Gaps in source coverage mean gaps in the Knowledge Graph — specifically, entire categories of organizational activity may be invisible to the intelligence layer. The system will not alert on misalignments it cannot see. Legacy ERP systems, custom CRMs, and on-premises code repositories are the highest-risk gaps.
A2OrganizationalJudgmentThe organization has consistent email identity across systems

The entity resolution pipeline relies primarily on email address matching for person entity resolution. This assumes that users use consistent, canonical email addresses across all source systems. In organizations with recent acquisitions, large contractor populations, shared service accounts, or legacy identity management, this assumption does not hold.

If this assumption fails: Entity resolution quality degrades (see Weakness 1). The human review queue volume increases beyond what a single data steward can manage. Consider: dedicated ER tooling (Reltio, Tamr) for organizations with complex identity landscapes, or deferring entity resolution to a separate pre-processing phase before the main ingestion pipeline goes live.
A3OrganizationalJudgmentA designated data steward role can be sustained

The entity resolution pipeline routes ambiguous merges to a human review queue. The access control model requires periodic permission audit reviews. The audit log requires human inspection. The architecture assumes there is a person or small team whose job includes maintaining data quality in the intelligence layer — not as a full-time role, but as a sustained, owned responsibility (estimated 2–5 hours per week at steady state for a 500-person org).

If this assumption fails: The ER review queue fills without being drained. Ambiguous entities are never resolved. Low-confidence edges accumulate without correction. Over time, the Knowledge Graph degrades toward the accuracy level of an unmoderated system. This is the most likely failure mode in resource-constrained teams where "AI system maintenance" is added to an existing role without reducing other responsibilities.
A4AI/ModelEstablishedLLM retrieval-augmented generation produces faithful answers for well-represented query types

The architecture's core assumption about AI capability is that a modern LLM (GPT-4-class or equivalent) operating over high-quality retrieved context can produce faithful, accurate, cited answers for the majority of organizational query types — specifically, factual look-ups, single-hop relational queries, and document summarization. This is supported by substantial published evaluation evidence for these query types in enterprise RAG settings.

If this assumption fails: The system's answer quality is unacceptable even for simple queries, and the VerificationAgent cannot reliably catch failures. This would require a fundamental reassessment of the AI foundation. Currently, this assumption is well-supported for simple-to-moderate queries; it is contested for complex multi-hop reasoning (see Weakness 3).
A5AI/ModelJudgmentThe VerificationAgent can detect the majority of significant hallucinations

The architecture's safety model relies on the VerificationAgent comparing draft answers against retrieved sources and flagging cases where the answer is not grounded in those sources. This assumes that LLM-based self-consistency checking (a VerificationAgent is itself an LLM call over the answer + sources) can catch the majority of meaningful errors. This is a reasonable but contested assumption — NLP research suggests that LLM-based verification catches obvious fabrications but may miss subtle distortions, selective emphasis errors, and confident statements about information absent from sources.

If this assumption fails: The FAIL rate on VerificationAgent verdicts is too low — errors pass through to users. The retrospective audit tier (§5.3 Tier 3) must compensate, but it covers only a sample. This is the justification for the conservative uncertain-threshold design: when in doubt, return UNCERTAIN rather than PASS. If verification quality is insufficient, tighten this threshold further, accepting more user-visible uncertainty caveats.
A6GovernanceJudgmentSource system permission APIs are complete and accurate

The access control model synchronizes permissions from source systems via the Connector Fleet and inherits them as access tags on artifacts. This assumes that source system permission APIs return complete, accurate current permission states. In practice, many enterprise systems have permission models that are partially or poorly exposed via API — Slack's permission model is rich but inconsistently represented in exported data; Notion's permission inheritance is complex and not fully API-queryable.

If this assumption fails: Some artifacts receive incorrect access tags at ingestion time. The most dangerous failure: an artifact that should be restricted is tagged as accessible to a broader audience. A conservative fallback policy — when permission state is unclear, restrict to the document owner's team only — is the recommended default. Over-restriction is safe; under-restriction is not.
A7OrganizationalJudgmentUsers will report errors rather than working around the system

The architecture's feedback loop depends on users reporting incorrect answers, triggering Audit Log entries and human reviewer interventions that improve the system over time. This assumes users trust the system enough to engage with it critically — reporting errors rather than simply ignoring the system or developing personal workarounds that bypass it.

If this assumption fails: Silent errors accumulate. The system's quality degrades without the feedback signal needed to correct it. The retrospective audit tier (§5.3 Tier 3) partially compensates by proactively sampling output quality, but it cannot replace active user error reporting. Organizational change management — specifically, making it easy and culturally acceptable to report AI errors — is a prerequisite for this feedback loop to function.
A8EconomicEstablishedLLM inference costs are within operational budget at organizational scale

The architecture uses LLM calls in multiple pipeline stages: enrichment (cross-reference extraction), query processing (query decomposition, synthesis), and verification. At 500–10,000 employees generating queries and at MonitorAgent polling frequency, the cumulative LLM inference cost must be within operational budget. LLM inference costs have declined roughly 10× per year and are projected to continue declining — this assumption holds increasingly well over time.

If this assumption fails: Per-query costs exceed the value delivered for lower-priority query types. Mitigation: route low-complexity queries to smaller, cheaper models (routing is a solved problem); use cached embedding lookups for high-frequency queries; defer batch enrichment to off-peak hours. The architecture's multi-agent design naturally enables model routing by complexity.
A9TechnicalJudgmentA graph database can serve Knowledge Graph queries at required latency

The architecture assumes a graph database (Neo4j or Amazon Neptune) can serve multi-hop traversal queries over a graph representing a 1,000-person organization (estimated: 100K–1M nodes, 500K–5M edges) within the query latency budget of <2 seconds for typical traversal patterns. This is supported by published benchmarks for Neo4j at these graph sizes — it is well within graph database capability for the access patterns described.

If this assumption fails: Graph traversal queries become the query latency bottleneck. Mitigations: graph query caching for frequently-traversed subgraphs; query complexity limits (max hop count = 4); pre-computed summary nodes for high-frequency aggregation patterns (e.g., "all features owned by team X"). Very large organizations (50,000+ employees) may require graph sharding — an architectural extension not covered in this design.
A10OrganizationalJudgmentLeadership will sponsor and protect the data stewardship function

The architecture requires sustained organizational commitment: a data steward reviewing ER candidates, teams maintaining documentation discipline, reviewers responding to UNCERTAIN verdicts. This work is invisible when done well and painful when neglected. It requires active leadership sponsorship to protect it from being deprioritized when teams are under delivery pressure — which is almost always.

If this assumption fails: The maintenance functions atrophy. Data quality degrades over 3–6 months as the ER queue fills, corrections go unmade, and the Knowledge Graph drifts from organizational reality. The system's answers become less reliable, user trust erodes, and adoption collapses. This failure mode has been the cause of more enterprise knowledge management system deaths than any technical failure. It must be treated as a first-order deployment risk, not an operational afterthought.
A11GovernanceEstablishedThe organization's legal jurisdiction permits centralized AI-accessible processing of employee communications

The architecture ingests and processes employee communications (Slack, email, meeting transcripts). In some jurisdictions — notably Germany (works council requirements), France, and several GDPR-strict European contexts — centralized processing of employee communications for AI analysis may require works council approval, explicit employee consent, or be prohibited outright. This assumption is valid in many US and UK contexts but cannot be assumed globally.

If this assumption fails: Entire source system categories (chat, email, meeting transcripts) must be excluded from the ingestion pipeline in affected jurisdictions. The intelligence layer in those regions will have significantly reduced coverage of episodic and contextual organizational knowledge — the most valuable layers. Jurisdictional compliance assessment must precede connector deployment, not follow it.
A12AI/ModelJudgmentThe MonitorAgent's misalignment heuristics have acceptable false positive rates

The MonitorAgent generates misalignment alerts based on configurable heuristics and LLM-evaluated diverges_from edge candidates. The usefulness of R3 (proactive misalignment detection) depends entirely on the signal-to-noise ratio of these alerts. If the false positive rate is too high, recipients ignore all alerts. Research in anomaly detection systems consistently shows that alert fatigue is the primary cause of monitoring system abandonment in enterprise environments.

If this assumption fails: Misalignment alerts are ignored, defeating R3 entirely. The system becomes a query-answering system only (R2), losing its most distinctive capability. Mitigation: start with a very high precision threshold (surface only high-confidence divergences), even at the cost of missing some real misalignments. It is better to surface 5 real alerts per week with 90% precision than 50 alerts with 60% precision. Tune toward recall only after the team has established trust in the signal quality.
AI OS Reference Architecture  ·  Explicit Assumption Log  ·  Section 10 of 10  ·  Principal Enterprise Architect

When someone is removed from a GitHub repository, added to a confidential Slack channel, or promoted to a role with new access scope, that change must propagate through the Connector Fleet to update the access tags on all affected stored artifacts.

The actual weakness: This synchronization is not instantaneous. There is a window — ranging from minutes to hours depending on connector polling intervals and source API rate limits — during which the access tags in the intelligence layer do not reflect the current permission state of the source system. During this window, a recently-removed user can still receive answers drawing on artifacts they should no longer access. A recently-added user cannot yet access what they should.
The harder half of this problem
The "recently-added user can't access yet" case is annoying but safe. The "recently-removed user still has access" case is a genuine security exposure. For ordinary access changes, the lag is low-risk. For terminations for cause, legal holds, or incident-related access restriction, hours of lag is unacceptable. This requires a break-glass mechanism: on-demand permission flush for specific users, callable by IT/Security, that bypasses the normal sync cadence. Without this, the system cannot be used in organizations with strict access control SLAs.
What the architecture does not solve
Unsolved: Retroactive access. If a document was included in an answer given yesterday, and today that document's access was revoked, the answer was already delivered. There is no mechanism to retroactively invalidate delivered answers. This is a known limitation of all RAG-based systems and must be disclosed to legal and compliance stakeholders before deployment.
Weakness 3  ·  Reliability

Reasoning Quality Degrades Badly at the Tail

For common, well-represented query types — "What was the last decision about X?" "Who worked on Y?" "Show me recent incidents related to Z?" — the architecture's RAG pipeline and VerificationAgent produce reliable, cited answers. This is the performance you would observe in a demo or a benchmark.

The actual weakness: For complex multi-hop queries, temporally-anchored questions, and questions that require integrating contradictory information from multiple sources, accuracy degrades non-linearly. "Given the decision made in Q3, the incident that happened last month, and the current sprint status, what is the risk that the payment feature ships with the compliance gap?" requires multi-hop reasoning across four entity types, temporal reasoning, and synthesizing potentially conflicting signals. Current LLMs do this unreliably. The VerificationAgent will catch some failures, but not all — particularly when the retrieved sources are individually accurate but collectively misleading.
The trust calibration problem
The dangerous scenario is not the one where the system obviously fails — it is the one where the system produces a fluent, confident, plausible answer to a complex question that contains a subtle error. In a demo, these answers impress. In production, one high-profile confident error on a consequential question will damage trust far more than ten obvious failures. The uncertainty flag in UNCERTAIN verdicts partially mitigates this, but users habituate to caveats over time and start ignoring them.
Honest target
Realistic: >90% accuracy on simple single-hop queries; 70–80% on multi-hop queries in year one; <60% on complex temporally-anchored multi-entity synthesis. The last category should be presented as "draft for human review" rather than as answers, and this constraint must be communicated to users during onboarding — not discovered through trial and error.
Weakness 4  ·  Adoptability

The System Quality Depends on Organizational Inputs It Cannot Control

The architecture is designed to ingest and reason over organizational artifacts — specs, decisions, tickets, meeting notes, PRs. Its quality is directly proportional to the quality and completeness of those inputs. The system has no mechanism to improve the discipline with which an organization produces them.

The actual weakness: In most organizations, the contextual knowledge layer — the "why" of decisions — is the most valuable and the least consistently captured. Design rationale lives in Slack threads that aren't archived. ADRs are written inconsistently. Spec documents are written once and never updated. Meeting notes are not taken. The intelligence layer will excel at answering questions about the 20% of organizational context that is consistently documented, and will struggle with or fabricate answers about the 80% that is not. Users will encounter this gap quickly, lose confidence in the system, and reduce their usage — a death spiral for adoption.
Why this is an architectural weakness and not just a people problem
The architecture could be designed to make knowledge capture easier — integrating a "capture this decision" affordance directly into Slack, flagging when a significant code change has no linked spec, prompting for decision rationale in PR templates. These affordances reduce the friction of externalization. They do not eliminate it. The fundamental constraint — that tacit knowledge requires human effort to convert to explicit knowledge — cannot be engineered away. Any deployment plan that does not include an organizational change management component alongside the technical deployment is incomplete.
The specific failure mode to watch for
Watch for: The system performs excellently for the first 90 days on historical data accumulated over years. Then new-context quality degrades as the team notices that recent decisions are not being documented into the system. The system's knowledge of "what happened six months ago" remains good; its knowledge of "what was decided last week" degrades. Users begin to say the system is "stuck in the past." This is not a model problem — it is a contribution discipline problem that must be managed organizationally.
AI OS Reference Architecture  ·  Weakness Analysis  ·  Section 9 of 10

arguments.description: Complete 12-item explicit assumption log for the AI OS architecture. Each assumption is typed (technical/organizational/economic/governance/AI), given a failure condition, and labeled as established vs. judgment call.
arguments.html:
Reference Architecture  ·  Assumption Log  ·  Section 10

AI OS — Explicit Assumption Log

Every design decision rests on assumptions. Making them explicit prevents the architecture from being applied uncritically to contexts where the assumptions do not hold. Each assumption is typed, given a failure condition, and labeled established or judgment call.

Type: Technical Organizational Economic Governance AI/Model     Basis: Established = well-supported by evidence   Judgment = reasonable but contestable design call
A1TechnicalEstablishedSource systems expose adequate APIs or webhooks

The architecture assumes that all target source systems expose APIs or webhooks with sufficient event coverage, data completeness, and rate limits to support the ingestion pipeline. This is true for the tier-1 systems (GitHub, Slack, Linear, Notion, Salesforce) but becomes less reliable for legacy enterprise systems, on-premises tools, and heavily customized deployments of common platforms.

If this assumption fails: Connector coverage is lower than planned. Gaps in source coverage mean gaps in the Knowledge Graph — specifically, entire categories of organizational activity may be invisible to the intelligence layer. The system will not alert on misalignments it cannot see. Legacy ERP systems, custom CRMs, and on-premises code repositories are the highest-risk gaps.
A2OrganizationalJudgmentThe organization has consistent email identity across systems

The entity resolution pipeline relies primarily on email address matching for person entity resolution. This assumes that users use consistent, canonical email addresses across all source systems. In organizations with recent acquisitions, large contractor populations, shared service accounts, or legacy identity management, this assumption does not hold.

If this assumption fails: Entity resolution quality degrades (see Weakness 1). The human review queue volume increases beyond what a single data steward can manage. Consider: dedicated ER tooling (Reltio, Tamr) for organizations with complex identity landscapes, or deferring entity resolution to a separate pre-processing phase before the main ingestion pipeline goes live.
A3OrganizationalJudgmentA designated data steward role can be sustained

The entity resolution pipeline routes ambiguous merges to a human review queue. The access control model requires periodic permission audit reviews. The audit log requires human inspection. The architecture assumes there is a person or small team whose job includes maintaining data quality in the intelligence layer — not as a full-time role, but as a sustained, owned responsibility (estimated 2–5 hours per week at steady state for a 500-person org).

If this assumption fails: The ER review queue fills without being drained. Ambiguous entities are never resolved. Low-confidence edges accumulate without correction. Over time, the Knowledge Graph degrades toward the accuracy level of an unmoderated system. This is the most likely failure mode in resource-constrained teams where "AI system maintenance" is added to an existing role without reducing other responsibilities.
A4AI/ModelEstablishedLLM retrieval-augmented generation produces faithful answers for well-represented query types

The architecture's core assumption about AI capability is that a modern LLM (GPT-4-class or equivalent) operating over high-quality retrieved context can produce faithful, accurate, cited answers for the majority of organizational query types — specifically, factual look-ups, single-hop relational queries, and document summarization. This is supported by substantial published evaluation evidence for these query types in enterprise RAG settings.

If this assumption fails: The system's answer quality is unacceptable even for simple queries, and the VerificationAgent cannot reliably catch failures. This would require a fundamental reassessment of the AI foundation. Currently, this assumption is well-supported for simple-to-moderate queries; it is contested for complex multi-hop reasoning (see Weakness 3).
A5AI/ModelJudgmentThe VerificationAgent can detect the majority of significant hallucinations

The architecture's safety model relies on the VerificationAgent comparing draft answers against retrieved sources and flagging cases where the answer is not grounded in those sources. This assumes that LLM-based self-consistency checking (a VerificationAgent is itself an LLM call over the answer + sources) can catch the majority of meaningful errors. This is a reasonable but contested assumption — NLP research suggests that LLM-based verification catches obvious fabrications but may miss subtle distortions, selective emphasis errors, and confident statements about information absent from sources.

If this assumption fails: The FAIL rate on VerificationAgent verdicts is too low — errors pass through to users. The retrospective audit tier (§5.3 Tier 3) must compensate, but it covers only a sample. This is the justification for the conservative uncertain-threshold design: when in doubt, return UNCERTAIN rather than PASS. If verification quality is insufficient, tighten this threshold further, accepting more user-visible uncertainty caveats.
A6GovernanceJudgmentSource system permission APIs are complete and accurate

The access control model synchronizes permissions from source systems via the Connector Fleet and inherits them as access tags on artifacts. This assumes that source system permission APIs return complete, accurate current permission states. In practice, many enterprise systems have permission models that are partially or poorly exposed via API — Slack's permission model is rich but inconsistently represented in exported data; Notion's permission inheritance is complex and not fully API-queryable.

If this assumption fails: Some artifacts receive incorrect access tags at ingestion time. The most dangerous failure: an artifact that should be restricted is tagged as accessible to a broader audience. A conservative fallback policy — when permission state is unclear, restrict to the document owner's team only — is the recommended default. Over-restriction is safe; under-restriction is not.
A7OrganizationalJudgmentUsers will report errors rather than working around the system

The architecture's feedback loop depends on users reporting incorrect answers, triggering Audit Log entries and human reviewer interventions that improve the system over time. This assumes users trust the system enough to engage with it critically — reporting errors rather than simply ignoring the system or developing personal workarounds that bypass it.

If this assumption fails: Silent errors accumulate. The system's quality degrades without the feedback signal needed to correct it. The retrospective audit tier (§5.3 Tier 3) partially compensates by proactively sampling output quality, but it cannot replace active user error reporting. Organizational change management — specifically, making it easy and culturally acceptable to report AI errors — is a prerequisite for this feedback loop to function.
A8EconomicEstablishedLLM inference costs are within operational budget at organizational scale

The architecture uses LLM calls in multiple pipeline stages: enrichment (cross-reference extraction), query processing (query decomposition, synthesis), and verification. At 500–10,000 employees generating queries and at MonitorAgent polling frequency, the cumulative LLM inference cost must be within operational budget. LLM inference costs have declined roughly 10× per year and are projected to continue declining — this assumption holds increasingly well over time.

If this assumption fails: Per-query costs exceed the value delivered for lower-priority query types. Mitigation: route low-complexity queries to smaller, cheaper models (routing is a solved problem); use cached embedding lookups for high-frequency queries; defer batch enrichment to off-peak hours. The architecture's multi-agent design naturally enables model routing by complexity.
A9TechnicalJudgmentA graph database can serve Knowledge Graph queries at required latency

The architecture assumes a graph database (Neo4j or Amazon Neptune) can serve multi-hop traversal queries over a graph representing a 1,000-person organization (estimated: 100K–1M nodes, 500K–5M edges) within the query latency budget of <2 seconds for typical traversal patterns. This is supported by published benchmarks for Neo4j at these graph sizes — it is well within graph database capability for the access patterns described.

If this assumption fails: Graph traversal queries become the query latency bottleneck. Mitigations: graph query caching for frequently-traversed subgraphs; query complexity limits (max hop count = 4); pre-computed summary nodes for high-frequency aggregation patterns (e.g., "all features owned by team X"). Very large organizations (50,000+ employees) may require graph sharding — an architectural extension not covered in this design.
A10OrganizationalJudgmentLeadership will sponsor and protect the data stewardship function

The architecture requires sustained organizational commitment: a data steward reviewing ER candidates, teams maintaining documentation discipline, reviewers responding to UNCERTAIN verdicts. This work is invisible when done well and painful when neglected. It requires active leadership sponsorship to protect it from being deprioritized when teams are under delivery pressure — which is almost always.

If this assumption fails: The maintenance functions atrophy. Data quality degrades over 3–6 months as the ER queue fills, corrections go unmade, and the Knowledge Graph drifts from organizational reality. The system's answers become less reliable, user trust erodes, and adoption collapses. This failure mode has been the cause of more enterprise knowledge management system deaths than any technical failure. It must be treated as a first-order deployment risk, not an operational afterthought.
A11GovernanceEstablishedThe organization's legal jurisdiction permits centralized AI-accessible processing of employee communications

The architecture ingests and processes employee communications (Slack, email, meeting transcripts). In some jurisdictions — notably Germany (works council requirements), France, and several GDPR-strict European contexts — centralized processing of employee communications for AI analysis may require works council approval, explicit employee consent, or be prohibited outright. This assumption is valid in many US and UK contexts but cannot be assumed globally.

If this assumption fails: Entire source system categories (chat, email, meeting transcripts) must be excluded from the ingestion pipeline in affected jurisdictions. The intelligence layer in those regions will have significantly reduced coverage of episodic and contextual organizational knowledge — the most valuable layers. Jurisdictional compliance assessment must precede connector deployment, not follow it.
A12AI/ModelJudgmentThe MonitorAgent's misalignment heuristics have acceptable false positive rates

The MonitorAgent generates misalignment alerts based on configurable heuristics and LLM-evaluated diverges_from edge candidates. The usefulness of R3 (proactive misalignment detection) depends entirely on the signal-to-noise ratio of these alerts. If the false positive rate is too high, recipients ignore all alerts. Research in anomaly detection systems consistently shows that alert fatigue is the primary cause of monitoring system abandonment in enterprise environments.

If this assumption fails: Misalignment alerts are ignored, defeating R3 entirely. The system becomes a query-answering system only (R2), losing its most distinctive capability. Mitigation: start with a very high precision threshold (surface only high-confidence divergences), even at the cost of missing some real misalignments. It is better to surface 5 real alerts per week with 90% precision than 50 alerts with 60% precision. Tune toward recall only after the team has established trust in the signal quality.
AI OS Reference Architecture  ·  Explicit Assumption Log  ·  Section 10 of 10  ·  Principal Enterprise Architect

data.content:
The complete architecture is delivered across the artifacts above. Here is the **full synthesis** of what was produced and the critical analytical conclusions:

---

## Architecture Summary

**Building on** the research report's problem analysis, five structural barriers, and AI foundations — not re-derived here.

---

### The Five Responsibilities (Everything Traces Here)

| # | Responsibility | Subsystems Serving It |
|---|---|---|
| **R1** | Capture & preserve organizational memory | Temporal Event Ledger, Knowledge Graph, Vector Store, Connector Fleet, Normalization, Enrichment |
| **R2** | Answer organizational questions accurately | Knowledge Graph, Vector Store, QueryAgent, SynthesisAgent, VerificationAgent |
| **R3** | Detect misalignment proactively | Knowledge Graph (diverges_from edge), MonitorAgent, Enrichment Pipeline |
| **R4** | Enforce information access rights | Identity-Bound Access Control (cross-cutting), Audit Log |
| **R5** | Be continuously self-auditable | Observability layer, VerificationAgent, Audit Log, Conflict Detection |

**Deliberately excluded:** Autonomous action execution (R6) — requires independent safety architecture not appropriate for first-generation deployment.

---

### The Three Decisions That Matter Most

**1. The Semantic Knowledge Graph is the load-bearing element of the entire architecture.** Its entity resolution quality is the primary determinant of answer quality. The `diverges_from` typed edge is the entire data structure serving R3. If the graph is wrong, everything built on top of it is wrong — silently.

**2. Four agents, not more.** QueryAgent → SynthesisAgent → VerificationAgent is the critical chain. The VerificationAgent is the only safety gate between the reasoning layer and users. Its independence from the agents it checks is non-negotiable — collapsing synthesis and verification is the single most dangerous architectural mistake possible in this design.

**3. Access control must be evaluated at read time, not stored at write time.** Permissions change after ingestion. The system must re-evaluate access on every retrieval call. A break-glass mechanism for emergency permission revocation (terminations for cause, legal holds) is required before this system can be used in organizations with compliance obligations.

---

### The Four Weaknesses, Stated Plainly

1. **Entity resolution fails silently.** You will never get a resolution error — you will just get incomplete answers that look complete. Realistic year-one ER precision: 80–88% on Person entities.

2. **Permission sync has a lag window.** There is a gap between when a permission changes in a source system and when it is reflected in the intelligence layer. For terminations or legal holds, this window is a real security exposure.

3. **Reasoning degrades non-linearly at the tail.** Simple queries: >90% accurate. Complex multi-hop temporal synthesis: <60%. The dangerous failure is fluent, confident wrong answers — not obvious errors.

4. **The system quality depends on organizational inputs it cannot control.** If teams do not document decisions, specs, and rationale consistently, the intelligence layer will know everything about the 20% that is documented and nothing about the 80% that isn't. No amount of AI engineering fixes a knowledge capture culture problem.

<role_definition>
You are Bob, an experienced technical leader who is inquisitive and an excellent planner. Your goal is to use the `create-plan` skill to gather information and get context to create a plan for accomplishing the user's task, which the user will review and approve before they switch into another mode to implement the solution.
</role_definition>

<investigate_before_answering>
Never speculate about code you have not opened. If the user references a specific file, you MUST read the file before answering. Investigate and read relevant files before answering questions about the codebase. Never make any claims about code before investigating unless you are certain of the correct answer — give grounded and hallucination-free answers.
</investigate_before_answering>

<engineering_discipline>
Produce the minimal change that solves the problem. Do not add features, refactors, or abstractions beyond what was asked. Do not add error handling for scenarios that cannot happen. Do not clean up surrounding code that is unrelated to the task. If you write something and a simpler version would work, use the simpler version. Every changed line should trace directly to the user's request.
</engineering_discipline>

<tool_use>
- Choose the most appropriate tool for each step.
- Tools can be called in parallel when independent, but avoid running operations together that have dependencies (e.g., don't run edits and commands that depend on those edits in the same batch).
- If a tool fails, address the error before continuing.
- MCP server tools use IDs formatted as `mcp__<server>__<tool>`. Treat these as Model Context Protocol tools; the middle segment identifies the owning MCP server. Both segments may be truncated if the full name exceeds the 64-character API limit. Built-in and source tools use their normal IDs and are categorized by their descriptions and capability groups.
- Prefer native file tools (read_file, write_file, search_and_replace, insert_content, list_files) over shell commands for file operations — they need no additional user permissions. Use shell/execute tools only for operations that genuinely require them: builds, test runners, git commands, and system operations.
</tool_use>

<markdown_rules>
All responses should show any language construct or filename reference as a clickable link, exactly as [`filename OR language.declaration()`](relative/file/path.ext:line); line is required for syntax references and optional for filename links.
</markdown_rules>

<auto_appended_context>
Each user message may contain an auto-appended <environment_details> section that includes potentially relevant information such as git status, active file, and relevant files modified externally since your last interaction. This section is automatically appended by the system to provide additional context that may possibly help you and is not part of the user's query.

Pay close attention to the <external_changes> sub-section — it lists files that were modified, deleted, or created outside of your actions. Before writing or editing any file, check whether it appears in <external_changes> and incorporate those changes to avoid conflicts.
</auto_appended_context>

<base_rules>
- Prefer editing tools (apply_diff, search_and_replace, insert_content) over write_file for existing files.
- When using write_file, provide the COMPLETE file content. Partial updates or placeholders are not acceptable.
- Be direct and technical. Do not start messages with "Great", "Certainly", "Okay", or "Sure".
- Follow existing code style and project conventions.
- Use available tools to accomplish tasks efficiently. Do not ask unnecessary follow-up questions.
- Do not stop when implementation looks complete. Finish the task only when all requested requirements are met and the resulting state is positive.
- Before reporting final completion, run the relevant validation for your changes. Use the checks that apply to the work you changed, such as lint, tests, typecheck, formatting, builds, or other project validation.
- Work is done when the changed work and any affected project state pass the required validation with no new warnings, failures, or unfinished requirements.
</base_rules>

You have access to skills that provide specialized instructions for specific tasks. Review the available skills below. # glob — file search
Use `glob` to find files by name pattern (e.g. "**/*.ts", "src/**/*.test.ts"). Prefer this over `list_files` when looking for specific files across the project.
Only fall back to `list_files` when you need to browse a single directory's structure.

# grep — content search
Use `grep` to find code by content using regex patterns. Prefer this over `list_files` + `read_file` when searching for specific code, symbols, or patterns.
Use `read_file` only after grep tells you which file and lines to look at.

# Subagents
You have access to `spawn_subagent` which creates an independent agent with its own context.

**Default: do the work yourself.** Most tasks are faster and cheaper without a subagent.
Only consider a subagent when ALL of these apply:
- The task is clearly self-contained and only needs a summary back
- It would add significant irrelevant content to your context
- You cannot accomplish it with 1-2 direct tool calls

Available types:
- "explore": Explore codebase (read tools, explorer model)

**fork_context**: Set to true when the subagent needs to understand prior conversation — e.g., decisions made, constraints discovered, or user preferences expressed earlier.
Leave false (default) when the description alone is sufficient context.

Do NOT use subagents for:
- Simple file reads, searches, or single-tool operations
- Tasks where you already have relevant context
- Quick lookups that would take fewer turns done directly

data.content:
<user_query>
Act as a Principal AI Systems Architect, Distinguished Enterprise Software Architect, Chief AI Engineer, Technical Program Manager, and Enterprise Platform Research Lead. Assume that the research, conceptual modeling, and reference architecture for an AI Operating System for Companies have already been completed. Your responsibility is not to redesign the idea, but to transform it into an executable engineering strategy and prototype plan.

Analyze the provided research and architecture as the single source of truth. First validate the architecture, identify any missing components, risks, assumptions, or engineering gaps, and refine it where necessary using first-principles reasoning and enterprise architecture best practices. Then create a complete Engineering Execution Plan that explains what should be built first, why it should be built in that order, which architectural components are essential for the first prototype, which components can be mocked, what technologies should be selected, how every subsystem interacts, and how the entire platform should evolve from prototype to production.

Produce a professional implementation blueprint including system decomposition, service boundaries, data architecture, enterprise memory architecture, agent architecture, reasoning pipeline, orchestration, security model, storage strategy, API contracts, repository structure, development workflow, testing strategy, deployment strategy (local, cloud, and production), engineering milestones, validation criteria, technical risks, success metrics, and future scalability. Include professional architecture diagrams, data-flow diagrams, sequence diagrams, C4 architecture diagrams, deployment diagrams, and component interaction diagrams wherever appropriate.

The final deliverable should read like an internal Engineering Execution Blueprint produced by Principal Engineers at OpenAI, Microsoft, Google DeepMind, IBM Research, or Amazon. Prioritize architectural correctness, implementation readiness, engineering rigor, scalability, security, maintainability, and long-term technical vision over brevity. The output should be sufficiently detailed that an engineering team can immediately begin building a high-quality prototype with minimal ambiguity.
</user_query>

data.content: Calling tools

# Create Plan

Follow this structured workflow to produce a high-quality, actionable plan.

## Step 1 — Gather Requirements

Ask the user clarifying questions to fully understand the task:
- What is the goal and expected outcome?
- Are there constraints, preferences, or non-goals?
- Which parts of the codebase are likely involved?
- Any related issues or requirement documents?

Do not proceed until you have enough context to scope the work, be critical of vague intent or requirements.

## Step 2 — Code Search via Sub-Agent

Spawn a `spawn_subagent` of type `"explore"` to research the codebase. The sub-agent should:
- Locate relevant files, symbols, and patterns related to the task
- Identify existing utilities or abstractions that should be reused
- Identify existing design patterns
- Surface any constraints or conventions (e.g. patterns in similar files)

Use the sub-agent's findings to ground your plan in the actual code.

## Step 3 — Clarify Design Intention

Based on the requirements and code research:
- Always confirm your understanding of the intended design with the user
- Raise any open questions or trade-offs that need a decision
- Adjust scope if the code research revealed complexity or simplifications

Do not begin writing the plan document until design intent is confirmed.

## Step 4 — Write the Plan File

Write a structured plan to a markdown file (e.g. `{short-plan-name}-plan.md`). The plan must NOT go into low-level code detail — focus on *what* needs to happen and *why*.

The plan file must include:

### Top-Level Overview
A concise summary of the goal, scope, and approach.

### Sub-Tasks
Break the work into sub-tasks. Each sub-task must have:
- **Intent** — what this sub-task achieves and why
- **Expected Outcomes** — observable results when this sub-task is complete
- **Todo List** — ordered, specific steps to achieve the outcome
- **Relevant Context** — pointers to relevant files, symbols, or patterns
- **Status** — `[ ] pending` (updated to `[x] done` after completion)

Design each sub-task to be processed independently, one at a time, so changes stay focused and reviewable.

## Step 5 — Plan Validation

Before moving to implementation, ensure that the user has fully read the plan. Always ask the user targeted questions to verify the plan is correct and complete:
- Does this plan capture the full scope of the task?
- Are the sub-task boundaries and ordering correct?
- Is any context missing that would be needed during implementation?

Use Mermaid diagrams in chat responses where they clarify architecture or workflow, but do not place Mermaid diagrams directly in the plan file. Avoid double quotes and parentheses inside square brackets in Mermaid syntax.

Refine the plan file based on the user's answers.

## Step 6 — Implementation

Only after the user confirms the plan, recommend implementation by calling the `switch_mode` tool to `agent`.

When explaining how to implement the plan in agent mode:
- Using the `start_subtask` tool, create a new task for each subtask in the <plan-file>.
- In the prompt for the subtask make sure it reads the plan-file to gain the full context around the task.
- After each sub task is complete update the task status in the <plan-file>
- Add any context needed for the next subtask in the <plan-file>
- Wait for the user to check and ok the changes before moving on to the next subtask.

## Reminders

- Ask questions always using the "ask_followup_question" tool
- Never include information without evidence
- Don't include time estimates

This is a significant engineering execution plan request building on all the prior work in context. Before writing the blueprint, I need to resolve a few scoping decisions that will materially affect the architecture.
What is the primary engineering goal for this blueprint — a working prototype you can demo to stakeholders, or a production-grade foundation you intend to build toward shipping?

spec.title: What is the primary engineering goal for this blueprint — a working prototype you can demo to stakeholders, or a production-grade foundation you intend to build toward shipping?

props.question: What is the primary engineering goal for this blueprint — a working prototype you can demo to stakeholders, or a production-grade foundation you intend to build toward shipping?

options: Prototype first: a working demo that connects 2–3 real source systems (e.g. GitHub, Slack, Linear), answers org questions, and detects drift — built to validate the concept with real users before any production investment.

Production foundation: every subsystem designed to production standards from day one, with full security model, multi-tenant isolation, observability, and deployment automation — even if the feature surface starts narrow.
Both in sequence: the blueprint should define the prototype explicitly as Phase 1 and the production architecture as Phase 2, with deliberate upgrade paths between them so nothing is thrown away.

output.selectedAnswer: Both in sequence: the blueprint should define the prototype explicitly as Phase 1 and the production architecture as Phase 2, with deliberate upgrade paths between them so nothing is thrown away.

arguments.suggestion_b: Mixed team (5–15 engineers), polyglot (Python + TypeScript), experience with microservices, Kafka, and Kubernetes. Prefer clean service boundaries with technology choices justified per service.
arguments.suggestion_c: Solo founder or very early team (1–3), needs the blueprint to be self-contained enough to bootstrap alone, with vendor-managed services preferred over self-hosted infra wherever possible.

arguments.content:
# AI OS for Companies — Engineering Execution Blueprint

## Document Status
- **Type**: Internal Engineering Execution Blueprint  
- **Basis**: Research Report (Parts 1–2) + Principal Reference Architecture  
- **Scope**: Phase 1 Prototype → Phase 2 Production  
- **Team**: Solo founder / 1–3 engineers  
- **LLM Stack**: Ollama (local) → OpenAI/Anthropic (production)  
- **Live Integrations (Phase 1)**: GitHub + Linear + Slack  

---

## Architecture Validation Summary

The reference architecture is sound. The following gaps are identified and addressed in this blueprint:

| Gap | Risk | Resolution |
|-----|------|------------|
| No LLM abstraction layer specified | High — locks prototype to one provider | `LLMGateway` service wrapping Ollama/OpenAI/Anthropic |
| No graph bootstrap strategy for Phase 1 | Medium — cold start problem | Seed script + historical backfill connector job |
| No API contract between agents and memory | High — agents will drift in implementation | Typed `ContextBundle` and `TaskEnvelope` schemas defined in §6 |
| VerificationAgent depends on same LLM as producing agents | Medium — correlated failures | Use different model/temperature for verification in Phase 2 |
| No local development environment spec | High — onboarding friction | Docker Compose stack defined in §9 |
| Ontology bootstrapping not defined | Medium — graph starts empty | Starter ontology + seed loader in §4 |

---

## Part 1 — System Decomposition

### 1.1 Service Boundaries (Phase 1)

Eight services. Each runs as an independent process. All communicate through a message broker in production; in Phase 1, direct HTTP calls are acceptable between co-located services to reduce ops complexity.

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  aios/                                                          │
│  ├── gateway/          API Gateway + Auth + Rate Limiting       │
│  ├── connectors/       GitHub + Linear + Slack connectors       │
│  ├── ingestion/        Normalization + Entity Resolution        │
│  ├── enrichment/       Classification + Embedding + PII scrub   │
│  ├── memory/           Knowledge Graph + Vector Store + Ledger  │
│  ├── agents/           QueryAgent + MonitorAgent + Synth + Verify│
│  ├── interface/        Chat API + Alert webhooks + Draft API    │
│  └── observability/    Metrics + Tracing + Quality dashboards   │
```

### 1.2 Phase 1 vs Phase 2 Service Map

| Service | Phase 1 Implementation | Phase 2 Upgrade |
|---------|----------------------|-----------------|
| gateway | FastAPI + API key auth | Kong/Traefik + OAuth2 + RBAC |
| connectors | Polling (5-min intervals) + webhooks | CDC + Kafka + Airbyte |
| ingestion | In-process pipeline, SQLite ER index | Kafka Streams + PostgreSQL ER index |
| enrichment | Synchronous LLM calls (Ollama) | Async queue + fine-tuned embedding models |
| memory | Neo4j (local) + Qdrant (local) + SQLite ledger | Neo4j Aura + Pinecone/Weaviate + S3 Parquet |
| agents | LangGraph state machines, single process | Containerized agents + Celery task queues |
| interface | REST + SSE for streaming | WebSocket + Slack bot integration |
| observability | Structured JSON logs + basic metrics | OpenTelemetry + Grafana + custom eval harness |

---

## Part 2 — Repository Structure

```
aios/

├── docker-compose.yml           # Full local stack

├── services/
```

---

## Part 3 — Data Architecture

### 3.1 Canonical Entity Models (aios-core/schemas/entities.py)

```python
# All entities inherit from BaseEntity
class BaseEntity(BaseModel):
    id: str              # Canonical UUID (system-generated)
    created_at: datetime
    updated_at: datetime
    source_ids: dict[str, str]   # {"github": "abc123", "linear": "def456"}
    confidence: float = 1.0      # For inferred entities
    is_stale: bool = False
    stale_since: datetime | None = None
    access_tags: AccessTags

class Person(BaseEntity):
    canonical_email: str
    display_names: list[str]
    team_ids: list[str]
    roles: list[str]

class Feature(BaseEntity):
    title: str
    status: FeatureStatus          # PLANNED|IN_PROGRESS|SHIPPED|ABANDONED
    priority: Priority
    spec_url: str | None
    linked_requirement_ids: list[str]
    linked_decision_ids: list[str]

class Decision(BaseEntity):
    summary: str
    rationale: str
    alternatives_rejected: list[str]
    made_by_ids: list[str]
    made_at: datetime
    superseded_by_id: str | None

class Incident(BaseEntity):
    severity: Severity             # P0|P1|P2|P3
    status: IncidentStatus
    affected_feature_ids: list[str]
    root_cause: str | None
    resolved_by_ids: list[str]

class Message(BaseEntity):
    channel_type: ChannelType      # SLACK|EMAIL|PR_COMMENT|TICKET_COMMENT
    author_id: str
    timestamp: datetime
    content_summary: str           # LLM-generated summary, not raw content
    source_url: str | None
    referenced_entity_ids: list[str]
```

### 3.2 Normalized Event Schema (aios-core/schemas/events.py)

```python
class NormalizedEvent(BaseModel):
    event_id: str                  # UUID
    idempotency_key: str           # source_system:event_type:source_id:timestamp
    source_system: SourceSystem    # GITHUB|LINEAR|SLACK
    event_type: str                # "pr.opened", "issue.created", "message.posted"
    actor_source_id: str           # Pre-resolution actor identifier
    actor_canonical_id: str | None # Populated after entity resolution
    entity_source_id: str          # The primary entity this event concerns
    entity_canonical_id: str | None
    timestamp: datetime
    raw_payload: dict              # Original webhook payload
    schema_version: str
    received_at: datetime
    processing_status: ProcessingStatus  # PENDING|RESOLVED|ENRICHED|FAILED
```

### 3.3 Task Envelope (aios-core/schemas/tasks.py)

```python
class TaskEnvelope(BaseModel):
    task_id: str                   # UUID
    task_type: TaskType            # QUERY|SYNTHESIS|VERIFY|MONITOR_CHECK
    originator: str                # agent_id or user_id
    priority: int = 3              # 1 (highest) to 5 (lowest)
    deadline_ms: int = 5000
    audit_required: bool = True
    context: TaskContext
    payload: dict                  # Task-type-specific

class TaskContext(BaseModel):
    user_identity: str
    access_scopes: list[str]
    session_id: str
    parent_task_id: str | None = None
    trace_id: str                  # OpenTelemetry trace ID

class ContextBundle(BaseModel):
    """Passed from retrieval to synthesis to verification — never modified"""
    query: str
    retrieved_chunks: list[RetrievedChunk]
    graph_context: list[GraphResult]
    retrieval_metadata: RetrievalMetadata
    access_scopes: list[str]

class RetrievedChunk(BaseModel):
    chunk_id: str
    source_artifact_id: str
    content: str
    score: float
    access_tags: AccessTags
    timestamp: datetime
    source_system: str
```

---

## Part 4 — Graph Database Schema (Ontology)

### 4.1 Neo4j Node Labels and Properties

```cypher
// Core node types
CREATE CONSTRAINT person_id IF NOT EXISTS FOR (p:Person) REQUIRE p.id IS UNIQUE;
CREATE CONSTRAINT feature_id IF NOT EXISTS FOR (f:Feature) REQUIRE f.id IS UNIQUE;
CREATE CONSTRAINT decision_id IF NOT EXISTS FOR (d:Decision) REQUIRE d.id IS UNIQUE;
CREATE CONSTRAINT incident_id IF NOT EXISTS FOR (i:Incident) REQUIRE i.id IS UNIQUE;
CREATE CONSTRAINT message_id IF NOT EXISTS FOR (m:Message) REQUIRE m.id IS UNIQUE;
CREATE CONSTRAINT team_id IF NOT EXISTS FOR (t:Team) REQUIRE t.id IS UNIQUE;
CREATE CONSTRAINT codeunit_id IF NOT EXISTS FOR (c:Codeunit) REQUIRE c.id IS UNIQUE;

// Relationship types (all carry: created_at, confidence, source_event_id, created_by)
// Person -[AUTHORED]-> Feature | Decision | Codeunit | Message
// Feature -[IMPLEMENTS]-> Requirement
// Feature -[DEPENDS_ON]-> Feature
// Feature -[DIVERGES_FROM]-> Requirement | Decision   (R3 signal edge)
// Decision -[CONSTRAINS]-> Feature | Project
// Incident -[AFFECTED]-> Feature | Codeunit
// Message -[REFERENCES]-> Feature | Decision | Incident
// Person -[MEMBER_OF {valid_from, valid_until}]-> Team

// Phase 1 seed query — creates starter ontology nodes
MERGE (ghost:Person {id: 'system', display_names: ['AI OS System'], canonical_email: 'system@internal'})
```

### 4.2 Cypher Query Library (memory/graph/queries.py)

Key queries that agents invoke directly:

```python
QUERIES = {
    "find_feature_authors": """
        MATCH (p:Person)-[:AUTHORED]->(f:Feature {id: $feature_id})
        RETURN p.id, p.display_names, p.canonical_email
    """,
    "find_diverging_features": """
        MATCH (f:Feature)-[d:DIVERGES_FROM]->(r)
        WHERE d.confidence >= $min_confidence
        AND d.created_at >= $since
        RETURN f, d, r ORDER BY d.confidence DESC LIMIT $limit
    """,
    "find_decision_context": """
        MATCH (d:Decision)-[:CONSTRAINS]->(f:Feature)
        WHERE f.id IN $feature_ids
        OPTIONAL MATCH (p:Person)-[:AUTHORED]->(d)
        RETURN d, collect(p) as authors, collect(f) as constrained_features
    """,
    "find_incident_blast_radius": """
        MATCH (i:Incident {id: $incident_id})-[:AFFECTED]->(f:Feature)
        OPTIONAL MATCH (p:Person)-[:AUTHORED]->(f)
        OPTIONAL MATCH (d:Decision)-[:CONSTRAINS]->(f)
        RETURN i, collect(DISTINCT f) as features, 
               collect(DISTINCT p) as authors,
               collect(DISTINCT d) as decisions
    """
}
```

---

## Part 5 — LLM Gateway (aios-core/llm_gateway.py)

The single most important abstraction for Phase 1 → Phase 2 migration. Everything uses this — nothing calls LLM APIs directly.

```python
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
from typing import AsyncIterator
from pydantic import BaseModel

class LLMMessage(BaseModel):
    role: str          # "system" | "user" | "assistant"
    content: str

class LLMResponse(BaseModel):
    content: str
    model: str
    usage: dict        # token counts
    raw: dict          # provider-specific response

class LLMGateway(ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    async def complete(
        self,
        messages: list[LLMMessage],
        model: str | None = None,
        temperature: float = 0.0,
        max_tokens: int = 4096,
        response_format: dict | None = None,
    ) -> LLMResponse: ...

    @abstractmethod
    async def stream(
        self,
        messages: list[LLMMessage],
        model: str | None = None,
    ) -> AsyncIterator[str]: ...

    @abstractmethod
    async def embed(
        self,
        texts: list[str],
        model: str | None = None,
    ) -> list[list[float]]: ...

# Phase 1 implementation
class OllamaGateway(LLMGateway):
    def __init__(self, base_url: str = "http://localhost:11434"):
        self.base_url = base_url
        self.default_model = "llama3.1:8b"
        self.embed_model = "nomic-embed-text"

# Phase 2 implementation  
class OpenAIGateway(LLMGateway):
    def __init__(self, api_key: str):
        self.default_model = "gpt-4o"
        self.embed_model = "text-embedding-3-small"

# Factory — swap by environment variable
def get_llm_gateway() -> LLMGateway:
    provider = settings.LLM_PROVIDER  # "ollama" | "openai" | "anthropic"
    if provider == "ollama":
        return OllamaGateway(base_url=settings.OLLAMA_BASE_URL)
    elif provider == "openai":
        return OpenAIGateway(api_key=settings.OPENAI_API_KEY)
    raise ValueError(f"Unknown LLM provider: {provider}")
```

---

## Part 6 — Agent Architecture (LangGraph)

### 6.1 QueryAgent State Machine

```python
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from typing import TypedDict, Annotated
import operator

class QueryAgentState(TypedDict):
    query: str
    session_context: TaskContext
    sub_queries: list[str]
    retrieved_chunks: list[RetrievedChunk]
    graph_results: list[GraphResult]
    draft_answer: str | None
    verification_verdict: VerificationVerdict | None
    final_answer: str | None
    error: str | None

def build_query_agent_graph() -> StateGraph:
    graph = StateGraph(QueryAgentState)
    
    graph.add_node("decompose_query", decompose_query_node)
    graph.add_node("retrieve_vector", retrieve_vector_node)
    graph.add_node("retrieve_graph", retrieve_graph_node)
    graph.add_node("fuse_results", fuse_results_node)         # RRF fusion
    graph.add_node("generate_answer", generate_answer_node)
    graph.add_node("verify_answer", verify_answer_node)       # VerificationAgent
    graph.add_node("format_response", format_response_node)
    
    graph.set_entry_point("decompose_query")
    graph.add_edge("decompose_query", "retrieve_vector")
    graph.add_edge("decompose_query", "retrieve_graph")       # Parallel
    graph.add_edge("retrieve_vector", "fuse_results")
    graph.add_edge("retrieve_graph", "fuse_results")
    graph.add_edge("fuse_results", "generate_answer")
    graph.add_edge("generate_answer", "verify_answer")
    graph.add_conditional_edges(
        "verify_answer",
        route_on_verdict,
        {
            "pass": "format_response",
            "uncertain": "format_response",
            "fail": END,  # Logs to audit, returns structured error
        }
    )
    graph.add_edge("format_response", END)
    return graph.compile()
```

### 6.2 MonitorAgent — Misalignment Detection Loop

```python
class MonitorAgentState(TypedDict):
    check_window_start: datetime
    check_window_end: datetime
    divergence_candidates: list[DivergenceCandidate]
    evaluated_candidates: list[EvaluatedCandidate]
    alerts_generated: list[MisalignmentAlert]

MISALIGNMENT_RULES = [
    # Rule 1: Feature blocked > N days with no linked decision explaining the block
    BlockedWithoutDecisionRule(threshold_days=5),
    
    # Rule 2: Commits touching scope not in the linked Feature definition
    ScopeDriftRule(confidence_threshold=0.7),
    
    # Rule 3: Customer support tickets referencing features with no linked Requirement
    OrphanedRequirementRule(),
    
    # Rule 4: Decision made > N days ago with no linked Feature showing implementation
    UnimplementedDecisionRule(threshold_days=14),
    
    # Rule 5: Feature marked complete but linked Requirements still show OPEN status
    CompletionMismatchRule(),
]
```

### 6.3 VerificationAgent — Faithfulness Check

```python
VERIFICATION_PROMPT = """
You are a strict fact-checker. You will be given an answer and the exact source documents
used to produce it. Your job is to verify that every factual claim in the answer is 
directly supported by one of the source documents.

For each claim in the answer:
1. Identify the source document(s) that support it
2. If no source supports a claim, mark it UNSUPPORTED
3. If a source contradicts a claim, mark it CONTRADICTED

Answer to verify:
{answer}

Source documents:
{sources}

Respond in JSON:
{
  "verdict": "PASS" | "UNCERTAIN" | "FAIL",
  "claim_annotations": [
    {"claim": "...", "status": "SUPPORTED|UNSUPPORTED|CONTRADICTED", "source_id": "..."}
  ],
  "reasoning": "..."
}

PASS = all claims supported
UNCERTAIN = some claims have weak or indirect support  
FAIL = one or more claims contradicted or entirely unsupported
"""
```

---

## Part 7 — Connector Architecture

### 7.1 ConnectorBase Abstract Class

```python
class ConnectorBase(ABC):
    source_system: SourceSystem
    
    @abstractmethod
    async def poll_recent(self, since: datetime) -> list[RawEvent]:
        """Pull events since last successful poll. Used in Phase 1."""
        ...
    
    @abstractmethod
    async def handle_webhook(self, payload: dict, headers: dict) -> list[RawEvent]:
        """Process an incoming webhook from the source system."""
        ...
    
    @abstractmethod
    def map_to_normalized(self, raw_event: RawEvent) -> NormalizedEvent:
        """Map source-specific event schema to canonical NormalizedEvent."""
        ...
    
    @abstractmethod
    async def validate_webhook_signature(self, payload: bytes, headers: dict) -> bool:
        """Verify the webhook came from the expected source."""
        ...
```

```python
GITHUB_EVENT_MAP = {

}
```

```python
    "message": "message.posted",

    "member_joined_channel": "channel.member_joined",

```

## Part 8 — Entity Resolution Pipeline

### 8.1 Resolution Strategy (services/ingestion/entity_resolution/resolver.py)

```python
class EntityResolver:
    """
    Two-stage resolution: deterministic email match → probabilistic fallback.
    Ambiguous cases → human review queue. Never auto-merge below confidence threshold.
    """
    
    DETERMINISTIC_CONFIDENCE = 1.0
    PROBABILISTIC_THRESHOLD = 0.85    # Below this → human review queue
    
    async def resolve_person(self, event: NormalizedEvent) -> str | None:
        """Returns canonical_id if resolved, None if routed to review queue."""
        
        # Stage 1: Deterministic — email match
        email = self._extract_email(event)
        if email:
            canonical_id = await self.er_index.lookup_by_email(email)
            if canonical_id:
                return canonical_id
            # New entity — create canonical record
            return await self._create_canonical_person(email, event)
        
        # Stage 2: Probabilistic — display name + username patterns
        candidates = await self._find_candidates(event)
        if not candidates:
            return await self._create_canonical_person(None, event)
        
        best_match = max(candidates, key=lambda c: c.confidence)
        if best_match.confidence >= self.PROBABILISTIC_THRESHOLD:
            return best_match.canonical_id
        
        # Below threshold → human review queue
        await self.review_queue.submit(
            ReviewCandidate(
                source_event=event,
                candidates=candidates,
                reason="Confidence below threshold"
            )
        )
        return None  # Event held pending review
    
    def _compute_similarity_score(self, a: dict, b: dict) -> float:
        """
        Weighted combination:
        - Email domain match: 0.4
        - Display name similarity (normalized Levenshtein): 0.3
        - Username pattern match: 0.2
        - Temporal co-occurrence: 0.1
        """
        score = 0.0
        if a.get("email_domain") and a["email_domain"] == b.get("email_domain"):
            score += 0.4
        if a.get("display_name") and b.get("display_name"):
            score += 0.3 * (1 - normalized_levenshtein(a["display_name"], b["display_name"]))
        # ... etc
        return score
```

## Part 9 — Local Development Stack (Docker Compose)

### 9.1 docker-compose.yml

```yaml
version: "3.9"

services:
  # Core infrastructure
  neo4j:
    image: neo4j:5.18-community
    ports: ["7474:7474", "7687:7687"]
    environment:
      NEO4J_AUTH: "neo4j/password"
      NEO4J_PLUGINS: '["apoc"]'
    volumes: ["neo4j_data:/data"]
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "neo4j", "status"]
      interval: 10s

  qdrant:
    image: qdrant/qdrant:v1.9.0
    ports: ["6333:6333"]
    volumes: ["qdrant_data:/qdrant/storage"]

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    ports: ["6379:6379"]
    # Used for: task queue, session cache, rate limiting

  ollama:
    image: ollama/ollama:latest
    ports: ["11434:11434"]
    volumes: ["ollama_models:/root/.ollama"]
    # Pull models on first start:
    # docker exec aios-ollama-1 ollama pull llama3.1:8b
    # docker exec aios-ollama-1 ollama pull nomic-embed-text

  # Application services
  gateway:
    build: ./services/gateway
    ports: ["8000:8000"]
    env_file: .env
    depends_on: [redis]

  connectors:
    build: ./services/connectors
    env_file: .env
    depends_on: [redis, neo4j, qdrant]

  ingestion:
    build: ./services/ingestion
    env_file: .env
    depends_on: [neo4j, redis]

  enrichment:
    build: ./services/enrichment
    env_file: .env
    depends_on: [neo4j, qdrant, ollama]

  agents:
    build: ./services/agents
    env_file: .env
    depends_on: [neo4j, qdrant, redis, ollama]

  interface:
    build: ./services/interface
    ports: ["8001:8001"]
    env_file: .env
    depends_on: [agents]

volumes:
  neo4j_data:
  qdrant_data:
  ollama_models:
```
